source_text:
Electrical risks have the potential to cause death and serious injury through direct or indirect contact with electricity. The most common risks are:

 Electric shock causing injury or death, if contact is made with energised parts.   
 Fire, arcing or explosion causing burns, for example when moisture or dust enter electrical equipment, or an explosive atmosphere is present.   
 Toxic gases causing illness or death.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy text describes common electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not state any requirement or process to manage those risks, does not assign responsibility to the PCBU, and does not reference compliance with Part 3.1 (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, implementation of controls, review/monitoring). Therefore, the policy is informational and does not fulfill the management obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to include a risk management framework for electrical risks aligned to Part 3.1: (1) assign responsibility to the PCBU and relevant roles; (2) require identification of electrical hazards across design, installation, operation, maintenance and testing; (3) require risk assessment where needed; (4) specify control measures using the hierarchy of controls (e.g., de-energisation/lockout-tagout, RCDs, isolation, guarding, inspection/testing, competency/training, PPE); (5) include procedures for high-risk scenarios (moisture/dust, explosive atmospheres); (6) require consultation, documentation, and periodic review/audit of controls and incidents.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies common electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out the specific preconditions required before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment. The PROVISION requires, before such work starts: (a) a competent-person risk assessment that is recorded; (b) the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (c) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly (subject to a limited exception); and (d) authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY does not mention conducting/recording a risk assessment, ensuring clear access/egress, marking/clearing/quick operation of isolation points, or formal authorisation/consultation. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s procedural requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded before work starts; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (and document the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include roles/responsibilities, checklists/forms for recording the assessment and authorisation, and verification steps before commencement.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text describes electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not state a control requirement to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised electrical work. The PROVISION imposes a specific duty on the business/operator to ensure persons are prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components while energised work is being carried out. Because the POLICY is only descriptive and lacks this mandated preventive measure, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements and controls for energised electrical work, including measures to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., prohibition/justification process for energised work, isolation where practicable, barriers/insulation/covers, exclusion zones, access control, supervision, PPE, tools, and procedures such as permits and testing).

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies general electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out operational requirements to manage a specific high-risk scenario addressed by the PROVISION: preventing persons/plant/things coming within an unsafe distance of overhead or underground electric lines, or (if not reasonably practicable) conducting a risk assessment and implementing controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. Because the POLICY lacks explicit commitments to maintain safe approach distances to electric lines and lacks the required fallback process (risk assessment + authority-aligned controls), it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to include explicit requirements to: (1) ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that no person/plant/thing comes within an unsafe distance of overhead or underground electric lines; (2) where safe distance is not reasonably practicable, require a documented risk assessment for the proposed work and implementation of control measures consistent with the assessment; (3) incorporate and comply with any applicable electricity supply authority requirements (e.g., consultation, permits, isolation/de-energisation, spotters, barriers, exclusion zones); and (4) reference applicable electricity safety legislation and define roles, responsibilities, and verification (training, supervision, and monitoring).

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not specify the required control measures mandated by the PROVISION: (1) testing electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised before electrical work; (2)(a) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (2)(b) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. Because these procedural obligations are not stated in the POLICY, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to determine energisation status; (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising; and reference or align with the applicable testing and verification method requirements (e.g., procedures consistent with the cited regulations).

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY describes key electrical risks (electric shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) and is intended to guide safety management around electrical hazards. The PROVISION defines what counts as “electrical equipment” for the purposes of a regulatory Part, including equipment above extra-low voltage and equipment in explosive/fire-risk atmospheres, and excludes certain vehicle propulsion-related equipment. These are functionally related because the definition determines the scope of what equipment the electrical-risk controls should apply to. However, the POLICY does not align with the PROVISION’s scope: it does not reference the extra-low voltage threshold, does not address the specific inclusion of cathodic protection systems, and does not acknowledge the vehicle propulsion exclusion. As written, the POLICY could be applied too broadly or miss regulated categories, so a compliance/scope alignment check fails.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly align scope with the regulatory definition by: (1) defining “electrical equipment” consistent with the PROVISION (including the > extra-low voltage threshold and inclusion of equipment in fire/explosion-risk atmospheres); (2) explicitly including active impressed current cathodic protection systems (AS 2832.1:2015) where applicable; (3) stating the exclusion for motor car/motorcycle propulsion-unit equipment where relevant; and (4) clarifying how the listed risks and controls apply within that defined scope.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set out the specific controls required by the PROVISION. The PROVISION imposes concrete obligations on a PCBU to (1) ensure regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-supplied equipment used in damaging environments (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals, dust), (2) for new/unused equipment, inspect for obvious damage before use, and (3)-(4) keep specified testing records (tester name, date, outcome, next test date; tag acceptable). The POLICY does not mention inspection/testing frequency, competent person requirement, pre-use inspection for new equipment, or recordkeeping requirements. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in the specified harsh/damaging environments; (2) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (3) retention of testing records until next test or removal/disposal; and (4) records to include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via equipment tags).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not include a control or requirement to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised electrical equipment during electrical work. The PROVISION imposes a specific duty on the business/operator to ensure de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out. Because the POLICY is only a hazard/risk statement and lacks this mandated control, it does not fulfill the obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a linked procedure) to require controls that prevent inadvertent re-energisation during electrical work, such as lockout/tagout (LOTO), isolation verification, securing isolation points, signage, controlled access, and a permit-to-work/authorisation process, with clear responsibilities and monitoring.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not set any control requirement about when energised electrical work may or may not be performed. The PROVISION imposes a specific obligation on the business/operator to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the listed exceptions applies (necessity for health and safety, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Because the POLICY does not include this prohibition/exception framework or an equivalent rule requiring de-energisation by default, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that electrical work on energised equipment must not be carried out unless one of the permitted conditions applies (health and safety necessity, necessary for proper work, required testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Add a documented decision/authorisation process to justify and record which exception applies before energised work proceeds, and clarify that testing may be included where allowed.

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text identifies key electrical risks (electric shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) but does not specify any control measures. The PROVISION requires that, in specified higher-risk circumstances (e.g., exposure to moisture/dust/heat/vibration, frequent movement, movement between locations, amusement devices), the PCBU must minimise electrical risk from socket-outlet supply by using an appropriate residual current device (RCD), with a ≤30 mA trip current for socket outlets ≤20 A, unless an explicit exemption applies (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer equivalent protection, or non-earthed isolated generator equivalent protection). Because the POLICY does not include these RCD requirements, thresholds, applicability conditions, or exemptions, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to require use of an appropriate RCD when electrical equipment is supplied via socket outlets in the circumstances listed (moisture/dust/heat/vibration/corrosives/mechanical damage environments; moved between locations; frequently moved; amusement devices). Specify that for socket outlets ≤20 A the RCD must trip at ≤30 mA. Document and control any exemptions (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer, isolated generator) by requiring verification that equivalent protection is provided. Add implementation details (responsibility, inspection/testing/maintenance of RCDs, and recordkeeping) to evidence the risk is minimised so far as reasonably practicable.

source_text:
Section 146 of the WHS Regulation defines “electrical work” as:

 connecting electricity supply wiring to electrical equipment or disconnecting electrical supply wiring from electrical equipment; or   
 installing, removing, adding, testing, replacing, repairing, altering or maintaining electrical equipment or an electrical installation.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text states that Section 146 defines “electrical work” and then lists only the inclusive definition (connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing/removing/adding/testing/replacing/repairing/altering/maintaining electrical equipment or an installation). The PROVISION text contains the same inclusive definition in subsection (1) but also includes explicit exclusions in subsection (2) (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, certain manufacturing/duct work under conditions, mounting equipment not related to connection, supervised assistance, and eligibility work). Because the POLICY purports to define the term but omits the exclusions that are part of the operative definition in the PROVISION, it is incomplete and could misclassify excluded activities as “electrical work,” creating a compliance/interpretation gap.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY definition to incorporate (or explicitly reference) the exclusions in Section 146(2), including the listed carve-outs and any stated conditions (e.g., supervision, de-energised wiring, not intended to be earthed). Alternatively, revise the POLICY to quote Section 146 in full or add a clear cross-reference stating that the definition is subject to the exclusions in subsection (2).

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines the scope of “electrical work” by referencing activities performed on “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation.” The PROVISION defines what counts as “electrical equipment” (and exclusions) for the purposes of the Part. This definition is functionally related because it determines whether the activities listed in the POLICY fall within the regulated category of “electrical work.” However, the PROVISION is definitional rather than an obligation or performance requirement, so a true/false compliance determination is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines the scope of “electrical work,” which includes work on an “electrical installation.” The PROVISION defines what constitutes an “electrical installation” (including what is and is not considered permanently electrically connected). This definition is functionally related because it determines whether activities described in the POLICY fall within “electrical work.” However, the PROVISION is definitional rather than an obligation or control statement, so a direct compliance determination (compliant/non-compliant) is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY TEXT defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (i.e., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing/removing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment or installations). The PROVISION TEXT imposes specific preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded, clear access/egress, isolation point marked/accessible/quick to operate, and authorisation after consultation). Because the provision’s obligations apply to a subset of the policy-defined activity (electrical work, specifically when performed energised), they are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary. However, the POLICY TEXT contains only a definition and does not address or evidence any of the required controls or processes in the PROVISION TEXT. Therefore, based on the provided texts alone, the requirements in (1)(a)-(d) and (2) are not met/covered, so it is non-compliant.
compliance.remediation: Implement and document a procedure for energised electrical work that, before work starts, ensures: (1) a competent person conducts and records a risk assessment; (2) the work area is cleared to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/egress, and can be operated quickly (or document applicability of the exception in (3)); and (4) formal authorisation is given after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Maintain records demonstrating these steps were completed for each job.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines the scope of “electrical work” (activities such as connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The PROVISION imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1, including risks arising from design, installation, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations. These are functionally related because the defined “electrical work” activities are key sources of electrical risks that must be managed. However, the POLICY only provides a definition and does not state any requirement to manage electrical risks, reference Part 3.1 risk management, or specify controls/processes. Therefore it does not demonstrate fulfillment of the PROVISION’s obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to include an explicit requirement that the PCBU must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks arising from electrical work (as defined), in accordance with WHS Regulation Part 3.1. Add a risk management process (identify hazards, assess risks, implement and maintain controls, review controls) and clarify applicability to design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing activities for electrical equipment/installations.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., disconnecting supply wiring, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment). The PROVISION imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. Meeting the PROVISION will typically require actions that fall within the POLICY’s definition of electrical work (disconnecting, testing, repairing). However, the POLICY text is only definitional and does not state any requirement that unsafe equipment must be disconnected/isolated or controlled from reconnection, nor does it assign responsibility or conditions for reconnection. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements and procedures to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is: (1) promptly disconnected/isolated from supply; (2) tagged/secured to prevent reconnection; (3) not reconnected until repaired or tested and verified safe by a competent/licensed person as applicable; and (4) otherwise replaced or permanently removed. Define criteria for 'reasonable grounds' of unsafety, assign responsibilities (PCBU/supervisors/workers), and include recordkeeping for isolation, testing/repair, and return-to-service.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The PROVISION text imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless specific necessity-based exceptions apply. Because the PROVISION’s obligation applies to activities that fall within the POLICY’s definition of “electrical work,” they are functionally related and a compliance check is relevant. However, compliance cannot be determined from the provided texts alone because no facts are given about whether energised electrical work is being performed and, if so, whether one of the permitted conditions (a)-(d) applies and is documented/justified.
compliance.remediation: If energised electrical work is occurring, implement and evidence a control process to ensure it is only performed when one of (1)(a)-(d) applies (including testing under reg 155 where relevant). Require documented justification for why de-energising is not reasonably practicable, confirm the specific exception relied upon, and ensure procedures permit energised testing only within the allowed circumstances.

This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if:
