source_text:
Electrical work does not include work on electrical equipment that is operated by electricity at extra-low voltage except electrical equipment that:

 is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or  is, or is part of an active impressed current cathodic protection system.

Appendix 1 What is not electrical work in CSIRO within the Electrical Safety Procedure lists the activities that are considered electrical work under Section 146 of the WHS Regulations.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is defining what is and is not “electrical work” for CSIRO purposes and explicitly references WHS Regulations s146, which is the same subject as the PROVISION’s definition and exclusions for “electrical work.” Therefore a compliance check is required. The POLICY’s exclusion is materially narrower/different than the PROVISION’s exclusions: it states electrical work does not include work on extra-low voltage equipment except in hazardous atmospheres or cathodic protection systems. The PROVISION, however, excludes a broader set of activities (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing certain components like fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing processes, certain duct/conduit work under conditions, mounting equipment not related to connection, supervised assisting, and eligibility training work). By not incorporating or aligning with these statutory exclusions, the POLICY risks misclassifying activities as “electrical work” (or failing to clearly recognize statutory carve-outs), creating inconsistency with the regulatory definition it cites.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY (and/or Appendix 1) to explicitly align with the WHS Regulations s146 definition and all applicable exclusions in the PROVISION. At minimum: (1) restate the full regulatory definition of “electrical work” and the complete list of exclusions; (2) clarify that CSIRO may impose stricter internal controls, but must not redefine the legal meaning—use language such as “for CSIRO control purposes we treat the following as electrical work” while acknowledging the statutory exclusions; (3) ensure Appendix 1 comprehensively maps each regulatory exclusion and any CSIRO-specific additions, with clear rationale and cross-references.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is defining what is (and is not) “electrical work” by carving out extra-low voltage equipment except for two specific cases (explosive/fire-risk atmospheres and active impressed current cathodic protection). The PROVISION, however, defines “electrical equipment” (not “electrical work”) and includes equipment > extra-low voltage as well as the same two special cases, and also provides an explicit exclusion for certain vehicle (motor car/motorcycle) propulsion-related equipment. Because the POLICY is using a definition-style statement that overlaps with the PROVISION’s scope and exceptions, a compliance alignment check is necessary. The POLICY is not compliant/aligned because it (1) omits the PROVISION’s broader inclusion of equipment used/operated at voltages greater than extra-low voltage, (2) does not incorporate the PROVISION’s explicit vehicle-related exclusion, and (3) conflates/does not clearly distinguish the statutory term being defined (electrical equipment) versus the operational term it is addressing (electrical work), creating a risk of misclassification against the regulatory definitions.
compliance.remediation: Revise the POLICY to clearly distinguish between the regulatory definition of “electrical equipment” and the definition/scope of “electrical work,” and ensure alignment with the PROVISION by: (a) incorporating the > extra-low voltage criteria where relevant; (b) explicitly addressing the vehicle (motor car/motorcycle) propulsion-related exclusion if applicable in your jurisdiction; and (c) cross-referencing the exact WHS Regulation provisions/definitions being implemented (and updating Appendix 1 references accordingly) to avoid definitional gaps or conflicts.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY defines what is and is not “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment) and references WHS Regulations s146. The PROVISION defines “electrical installation,” which is a key term used to determine whether equipment is part of an installation (e.g., permanently connected vs plug-and-socket) and therefore affects whether work may be classified as electrical work. A compliance check is necessary because the POLICY uses the term “electrical installation” but does not incorporate or align with the regulatory definition provided in the PROVISION. Specifically, the POLICY does not clarify what constitutes an “electrical installation,” including the important distinction that plug-and-socket connected equipment is not permanently connected, and that supply authority works are not relevant to determining electrical connection. This omission can lead to misclassification of work scope and incorrect application of the extra-low voltage exclusion and its exceptions.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or the referenced Appendix 1) to explicitly adopt or cross-reference the WHS regulatory definition of “electrical installation,” including: (1) the requirement for permanent electrical connection; (2) that plug-and-socket connected equipment is not permanently connected; and (3) that connection via supply authority works is not considered when determining electrical connection. Ensure the “electrical installation” term is used consistently with this definition wherever the extra-low voltage exclusion and hazardous-area exception are applied.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines/excludes what counts as “electrical work” (particularly extra-low voltage equipment, with exceptions) and points to an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. The PROVISION imposes specific mandatory controls when electrical work is performed on energised electrical equipment (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; SWMS content requirements; safety observer requirements and exceptions; ensuring proper use of equipment). Because the POLICY is part of determining scope of “electrical work” within the organisation, it is functionally related to whether and when the PROVISION’s controls must be applied. However, the POLICY does not include or reference the required controls for energised electrical work (competency/tools/PPE requirements, SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements, risk assessment exception). It only addresses classification/scope, so it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s operational obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or the referenced Appendix 1) to explicitly require that any energised electrical work is performed in accordance with the PROVISION: (1) only by a competent person with suitable, tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) under a SWMS that includes identification of the work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (3) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation, unless the work is testing only and a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk; and (4) include a requirement that the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE. Also ensure the scope definition (extra-low voltage exclusions and exceptions) is aligned to when these controls must be triggered.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines/excludes what counts as “electrical work” (particularly extra-low voltage work) and points to an appendix listing activities considered electrical work under WHS Regs s146. The PROVISION imposes specific preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded, area clear, isolation point marked/accessible/quick to operate, and authorisation after consultation). Because the POLICY governs the scope of what CSIRO treats as electrical work, it is functionally related to determining when the PROVISION’s energised electrical work controls must be applied. However, the POLICY does not include or reference the required pre-start controls for energised electrical work (risk assessment/recording, access/egress, isolation point requirements, authorisation/consultation, or the stated exception). Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or Appendix 1) to explicitly require, before any energised electrical work commences: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) the work area is clear for access/exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or document the subregulation (3) exception conditions); and (4) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include roles/responsibilities, evidence/recordkeeping requirements, and a clear trigger that these controls apply whenever energised electrical work is undertaken.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy text instead defines what is and is not considered “electrical work,” focusing on exclusions for extra-low voltage equipment with limited exceptions, and references an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. It does not state a requirement or process to manage electrical risks (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, control measures, maintenance/testing controls) nor does it reference compliance with Part 3.1 risk management requirements. Therefore, it does not demonstrate fulfillment of the broader duty to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to explicitly require management of electrical risks in accordance with WHS risk management (Part 3.1), including: identifying electrical hazards/risks (covering equipment and installations), assessing risks where required, implementing and maintaining controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, inspection/testing, RCDs, competent persons, exclusion zones), and reviewing controls. Clarify that the duty applies regardless of whether an activity is classified as “electrical work,” and cross-reference the organization’s risk management procedure and relevant electrical safety controls.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment and references to an internal appendix). The PROVISION imposes specific duties on a PCBU before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment: testing by a competent person to determine if it is energised, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Because the POLICY is setting the scope of activities that constitute electrical work, it is functionally related to when the PROVISION’s obligations apply. However, the POLICY does not state or require the mandated pre-work testing by a competent person, the “treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised” rule, or the earthing requirement for high-voltage exposed parts. Therefore, it does not demonstrate alignment with the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or Appendix 1) to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment: (1) the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after being de-energised. Include references to the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., per the relevant regulations/standard operating steps) and clarify applicability across the defined scope of “electrical work.”

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines the scope of what counts as “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation” for duties in the Division by limiting it to equipment/installations under the PCBU’s management or control. The policy defines what is and is not “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment) and references an internal appendix for activities considered electrical work. However, the policy does not incorporate or acknowledge the management/control limitation when referring to electrical equipment/installations (e.g., it could be read to apply to any such equipment/installations regardless of whether CSIRO has management or control). This creates a scope mismatch with the provision’s definition and could lead to over- or mis-application of requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly state that references to electrical equipment/electrical installations (and related determinations of what is electrical work) apply to equipment/installations under CSIRO’s management or control (or otherwise clarify how the procedure applies when equipment is not under CSIRO control, such as contractor-owned equipment). Update Appendix 1 and any related definitions to align with this scope.

source_text:
Electrical Safety Procedure Section 1 – HSE Risk Management

Prior to undertaking any electrical work or activities involving a possible electrical hazard that has the potential to cause injury or property/infrastructure damage an Activity Risk Assessment must be completed. A Safe Work Instruction (SWI) must be completed for recurring electrical work and energised electrical work.

Section 147 of the WHS Regulation specifies that an employer “must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace”. This includes electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace.

Section 2 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides the following guidance that may assist when completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA):

 different hazards associated with electrical equipment and installations;   
 considerations when assessing the nature and severity of risks associated with electrical hazards and electrical work; and   
 considerations when identifying and implementing control measures to eliminate or reduce the risks with associated with electrical hazards.

Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for information on risk assessment consideration for energised electrical work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it is intended to implement the duty to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The POLICY requires an Activity Risk Assessment prior to electrical work/activities involving electrical hazards and requires Safe Work Instructions for recurring and energised electrical work, aligning with the PROVISION’s requirement to manage electrical risks (including those arising from design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing). While the POLICY references WHS Regulation s147 and provides guidance sources (Code of Practice) rather than explicitly stating 'in accordance with Part 3.1', its controls (risk assessment and work instructions) are consistent with risk management obligations under Part 3.1.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) before electrical work involving possible electrical hazards and requires a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for recurring and energised electrical work. The PROVISION imposes specific preconditions before energised electrical work commences: (1)(a) a risk assessment conducted; (2) by a competent person and recorded; plus (1)(b) work area clear of obstructions; (1)(c) isolation/disconnection point clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly; and (1)(d) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY partially aligns on the need for a risk assessment and additional documentation (SWI), but it does not explicitly require that the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded, nor does it address the specific site/isolator access and labelling requirements, nor the authorisation/consultation requirement prior to commencing energised work. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s mandatory elements for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a risk assessment conducted before work starts, by a competent person, and recorded; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions for access/egress; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly (and note the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) a documented authorisation step after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace prior to commencing energised electrical work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets requirements for managing electrical risks and specifically references requirements for energised electrical work (e.g., requiring an Activity Risk Assessment and a Safe Work Instruction for energised work). However, it does not include several mandatory elements required by the PROVISION for energised electrical work: (1) it does not require energised work to be carried out by a competent person with suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) it does not require the work to be performed in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) nor specify the required SWMS contents (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review); (3) it does not require a safety observer with specified competence and recent (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation assessment, nor does it describe the limited exception where a safety observer is not required (testing only + risk assessment showing no serious risk); and (4) it does not require, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE. Therefore, the policy only partially addresses risk assessment/SWI concepts and does not meet the specific procedural obligations for energised electrical work in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (a) energised work be performed only by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (b) a SWMS (or equivalent document explicitly meeting SWMS requirements) be prepared for the energised work and include the required contents (work identification, hazards/risks, controls, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed); (c) a competent safety observer be present, with documented assessment within the last 12 months in rescue and resuscitation, and define the exception (testing only + risk assessment showing no serious risk); and (d) supervisory/verification measures to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is about managing electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions, which is functionally related to the PROVISION’s requirements for safe electrical work practices. However, the PROVISION imposes specific mandatory controls before and during electrical work: (1) testing electrical equipment by a competent person to determine if it is energised before work starts; (2)(a) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (2)(b) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. The POLICY text provided does not explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised' rule, and does not require earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the specific obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements to explicitly mandate: (1) before any electrical work on equipment, testing by a competent person to confirm whether the equipment is energised (with reference to applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage work, earthing of exposed parts after de-energising. Ensure these steps are embedded in SWIs/permit-to-work/ARA templates and training/competency requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related because it addresses risk management requirements for electrical work, including energised electrical work, which is directly governed by the PROVISION restricting when energised electrical work may be performed. However, the POLICY does not state the core legal requirement that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the specified necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Instead, it focuses on completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instruction, which are process controls but do not implement the prohibition/permission threshold required by the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work except where one of the permitted conditions applies (as per (1)(a)-(d)), and require documented justification that at least one condition is met before authorisation. Include a decision/approval step in the ARA/SWI for energised work that records which condition applies (including reg 155 testing where relevant) and confirms there is no reasonable alternative (e.g., isolation/de-energisation) where applicable.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes a general requirement to manage electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions, referencing WHS Regulation s147 (risk management) and the Code of Practice. The PROVISION imposes specific, mandatory duties to (1) regularly inspect and test certain socket-outlet supplied electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments by a competent person, (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, and (3) keep detailed testing records until next test or disposal. The POLICY does not explicitly require periodic inspection/testing of in-scope equipment, does not specify competency requirements for testers, does not require pre-use inspection for new equipment, and does not require retention/content of testing records (including next test due date or tagging). Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrably fulfill the PROVISION’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require: (1) a program of regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/chemicals/dust); (2) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (3) recordkeeping for each test until the next test or disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date; and (4) allow/require tagging as the record (or equivalent system). Define inspection/testing intervals and responsibilities, and integrate these requirements into ARA/SWI processes.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The policy text focuses on completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions for recurring and energised electrical work, and generally references managing electrical risks. However, it does not explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, access control, supervision) nor does it state an outcome requirement to prevent persons from creating electrical risk during energised work. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrably meet the specific obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components, such as: establishing exclusion zones and access control; installing physical barriers/guards or insulating covers; ensuring exposed live parts are shrouded/insulated where practicable; signage and spotter/supervision where needed; and verifying these controls are implemented and maintained for the duration of energised work. Include a clear responsibility statement that the PCBU must ensure persons are prevented from creating electrical risk during energised work, and require the ARA/SWI to document and verify these measures.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.
