source_text:
Electrical workers must be deemed competent to carry out electrical work.

Electrical work competencies are regulated by the states and territories, as a result your location will determine what types of competencies are required for the work you carry out.

Table 1 provides an overview of what licences are required in each state and territory for specific electrical work. If an electrical licence isn’t required, then competency must be demonstrated through the successful completion of a practical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO.

Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both address who is permitted/competent to perform “electrical work” and under what conditions (licensing/competency and supervision). The provision defines the scope of “electrical work” and explicitly lists exclusions and conditional allowances (e.g., supervised assistance, non-energised conduit work, eligibility/training work). The policy requires workers to be deemed competent and references state/territory licensing and CSIRO-agreed practical competency assessments, but it does not (in the provided excerpt) align its requirements to the provision’s definition and exclusions. In particular, it does not clarify that certain tasks are not “electrical work” (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, replacing light bulbs/fuses) and does not specify the supervision conditions under which unlicensed persons may assist or perform limited tasks. This creates a gap where the policy could over-restrict or misclassify work, and it does not demonstrate coverage of the provision’s conditional exceptions.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (and Table 1) to: (1) adopt or cross-reference the provision’s definition of “electrical work”; (2) explicitly list or reference the excluded activities that are not electrical work; (3) specify supervision and de-energised conditions for permitted non-licensed assistance/activities (e.g., direct supervision, no contact with energised equipment); and (4) map licensing vs competency assessment requirements to the defined scope so the policy’s competency/licensing controls apply only where the provision deems the activity to be electrical work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical workers to be deemed competent and references state/territory licensing or a practical competency assessment. The PROVISION imposes additional, specific duties for electrical work on energised electrical equipment: (1) the work must be carried out by a competent person who has suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) the PCBU must ensure (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use of that equipment; (3) the work must be performed in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specified content; and (4) a safety observer must be present with specified competence and recent (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation assessment, unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies. The POLICY does not address SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements/exception criteria, or the requirements around suitability/testing/maintenance and proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE. Therefore, the POLICY only partially aligns (competency concept) and does not demonstrate compliance with the full set of obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (and referenced Table 1/procedures) to explicitly require for energised electrical work: (1) use of suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (2) a process to ensure workers use such equipment properly (training/supervision/verification); (3) preparation and use of a SWMS meeting the provision’s content requirements; (4) a safety observer requirement including competence criteria and documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months; and (5) the defined exception process (testing-only plus documented risk assessment showing no serious risk).

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, before electrical work is carried out, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, with high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energisation. The policy only states that electrical workers must be deemed competent and references licensing/competency assessment requirements by jurisdiction. It does not require pre-work testing for energisation, does not specify that testing must be performed by a competent person for this purpose, and does not include the exposed-parts-as-energised rule or the high-voltage earthing requirement. Therefore, the policy does not fully address the operational safety obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) before any electrical work on electrical equipment, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised (with reference to applicable testing method requirements); (2) all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after being de-energised. Define/document who is considered a competent person for testing, required records of testing/verification, and jurisdictional variations if applicable.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical workers to be deemed competent (via licensing where required or a practical competency assessment). The PROVISION imposes additional, specific preconditions before energised electrical work commences: a risk assessment must be conducted (by a competent person) and recorded, the work area and isolation/disconnection point must be clear/labelled/quickly operable, and the work must be authorised after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY does not address these energised-work commencement controls (risk assessment/recording, access/egress and isolation point requirements, or authorisation/consultation). Therefore, while the POLICY partially aligns on the concept of competency, it does not demonstrate compliance with the full set of obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person before work starts; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or document applicability of the stated exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that certain electrical equipment be regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and that testing records be kept with specific required details (tester name, test date, outcome, next test date), potentially via a tag. The policy text only states that electrical workers must be deemed competent and that competency/licensing requirements vary by state/territory, with competency assessment where no licence is required. It does not establish a requirement for regular inspection/testing of in-scope equipment, does not address the exemption/inspection-for-obvious-damage requirement for new/unused equipment, and does not require creation/retention/content of testing records/tags. Therefore, the policy does not fully implement the obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) require regular inspection and testing of electrical equipment that is plug-in (socket outlet supplied) and used in hostile/damaging environments (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosives, dust); (2) specify that testing must be performed by a competent person (and define/align competency with state/territory requirements); (3) include the new/unused equipment rule—no initial test required but must be inspected for obvious damage before first use; (4) mandate recordkeeping until next test or disposal/removal, and require records/tags to include tester name, test date, outcome, and next due date; (5) reference that unsafe equipment must not be used.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

compliance.reasoning: Both texts address competency requirements tied to licensed work. The POLICY requires electrical workers to be deemed competent and, where a licence is not required, to demonstrate competency via a practical competency assessment agreed by CSIRO. The PROVISION establishes that a regulator may require a licence holder to undergo reassessment of competency for high risk work if there is a reasonable belief of incompetence. The POLICY does not address regulator-directed reassessment for licence holders (i.e., ongoing/triggered reassessment obligations) and instead focuses on initial licensing requirements and CSIRO-agreed assessments where no licence is required. This creates a gap: the POLICY does not ensure processes exist to comply with potential regulator directions for reassessment of licensed workers’ competency.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to include a requirement and process for responding to regulator directions for competency reassessment of licence holders (including triggers, responsibilities, timeframes, recordkeeping, and how reassessment outcomes affect authorization to perform work), in addition to the existing licensing/initial competency assessment requirements.

matches.target_title: 171 Competence of worker—general diving work—qualifications
matches.target_text:
(1) A person must not carry out any type of general diving work unless the person holds a certificate for general diving work, issued by a training organisation, that demonstrates that the person has acquired the relevant competencies for that type of general diving work.   
(2) This regulation does not apply in relation to incidental diving work or limited scientific diving work.   
(3) In subregulation (1), relevant competencies means the competencies specified in AS/NZS 2815 (Training and certification of occupational divers) that are relevant to the type of general diving work to which subregulation (1) applies.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision regulates when energised electrical work may be performed (only under specific necessity conditions) and places a duty on the PCBU to ensure it is not carried out otherwise. The policy text addresses only worker competency/licensing requirements by jurisdiction and does not include any controls or prohibitions regarding performing electrical work on energised equipment, nor the required necessity tests (health and safety necessity, need for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s requirement to prevent unauthorised energised work.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised electrical equipment unless one of the permitted conditions applies (as per (1)(a)-(d)), require documented justification/approval for energised work, and include controls for energised work (e.g., risk assessment, isolation/LOTO as default, testing-only allowances, and reference to testing requirements under reg 155).

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be carried out by a competent person and in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also adds a specific constraint: a person is not competent to perform a detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation unless they are qualified (or assisted by someone qualified) to inspect electrical installations. The policy addresses electrical worker competence and licensing/competency assessment by jurisdiction, but it does not (a) explicitly cover amusement devices/passenger ropeways, (b) require adherence to designer/manufacturer recommendations or a maintenance manual, or (c) include the specific requirement that detailed inspections involving electrical installations must be performed by a qualified electrical installation inspector or with qualified assistance. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require that for amusement devices and passenger ropeways: (1) maintenance, inspection and testing are performed by a competent person; (2) work is performed in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; and (3) any detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation is performed only by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person. Define/verify what 'qualified to inspect electrical installations' means per jurisdiction and ensure records of qualifications/assistance are maintained.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1). The policy focuses narrowly on ensuring electrical workers are deemed competent via licensing or practical competency assessment. While worker competency is a risk control that can contribute to managing electrical risks, the policy does not establish a broader risk management process (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, implementation of controls for design/installation/maintenance/testing, review of controls) as required by the provision and Part 3.1. Therefore, the policy only partially addresses the obligation and is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy to include a documented electrical risk management framework aligned to WHS Part 3.1, including: identification of electrical hazards/risks across design, installation, operation, maintenance and testing; risk assessment where required; implementation of controls using the hierarchy of controls (e.g., isolation, RCDs, lockout/tagout, testing and inspection regimes, safe work procedures); assignment of responsibilities (PCBU/management/supervisors/workers); training and supervision requirements; consultation and communication; incident reporting; and periodic review/audit of control effectiveness. Keep the competency/licensing requirements as one control within this broader system.

source_title: 4.2 Electrical work – who can do what?
source_text:
Electrical work, whether energised or de-energised, must only be carried out by a person who is deemed competent under applicable state/territory electrical legislation.

An electrical worker’s training and qualifications will impact the type of electrical work they can perform. Table 2 outlines what electrical work electrical workers in certain positions should be able to perform based on their qualifications and training.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY sets a rule about who may perform “electrical work,” while the PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and explicitly lists exclusions and conditional allowances (e.g., supervised assistance, certain non-energised conduit work, tasks like replacing a fuse/light bulb, plug-and-socket connection). The POLICY requires that all electrical work (energised or de-energised) be carried out only by a person deemed competent under applicable legislation, but it does not acknowledge that some activities are not “electrical work” under the PROVISION or that some activities may be performed by non-licensed/non-expert persons under specified conditions. As written, the POLICY is stricter and may incorrectly classify excluded tasks as requiring a competent electrical person, creating a mismatch with the legislative definition and scope.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to align with the legislative definition and exclusions by: (1) adopting the PROVISION’s definition of “electrical work” (or referencing it explicitly); (2) explicitly stating that activities excluded from “electrical work” under applicable legislation are not subject to the ‘competent person’ requirement; (3) adding conditions for permitted non-licensed participation (e.g., supervised assistance without contact with energised equipment; specified non-energised conduit/duct work under supervision; eligibility/training work) consistent with the PROVISION; and (4) updating Table 2 and any role-based permissions to reflect these distinctions.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because both address who may perform electrical work (competency requirements). However, the PROVISION imposes specific additional obligations for energised electrical work that are not reflected in the POLICY: (1) ensuring suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) requiring the work be performed in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specified content; (3) requiring a competent safety observer with recent (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation competency assessment, unless the testing-only and risk-assessment exception applies; and (4) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses the tools/PPE properly. Because the POLICY only states that electrical work (energised or de-energised) must be carried out by a competent person and references training/qualifications, it does not fully meet the PROVISION’s requirements for energised work.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (a) use of suitable tools, testing equipment and PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (b) preparation and use of a SWMS that identifies the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and implementation/monitoring/review steps; (c) a competent safety observer present with documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, unless the testing-only + risk assessment shows no serious risk; and (d) supervisory/verification measures to ensure workers properly use tools/testing equipment/PPE so far as reasonably practicable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs who may perform electrical work (must be a competent person under legislation) and references qualifications/training. The PROVISION imposes additional, specific safety duties on the PCBU before and during electrical work: (1) ensure the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine if it is energised before work starts; (2) ensure exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) ensure high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation. The POLICY does not address mandatory pre-work testing to confirm energisation status, the 'treat as energised until proven otherwise' rule, or the earthing requirement for high-voltage exposed parts. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully cover the obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine whether it is energised (with reference to applicable testing procedures/standards); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) earthing of each high-voltage exposed part after de-energisation. Define roles/responsibilities (PCBU vs worker), include verification/recordkeeping requirements, and align with the referenced regulations on testing methods.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires that all electrical work (energised or de-energised) is only carried out by a person deemed competent under applicable electrical legislation, and notes that qualifications/training determine what work can be performed. The PROVISION imposes specific preconditions on commencing energised electrical work (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation point marked/accessible/operable, and authorisation after consultation) and requires the risk assessment to be conducted by a competent person and recorded. While the POLICY addresses competency, it does not address the PROVISION’s additional mandatory controls for energised work (risk assessment and recording, site/access requirements, isolation point requirements, and authorisation/consultation). Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (and supporting procedures) for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person before work starts; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (or define the limited exception conditions); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Include roles/responsibilities, recordkeeping requirements, and verification/audit steps.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty on the PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the listed exceptions applies (necessity for health and safety, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy addresses who may perform electrical work (competency/qualifications) but does not include the required prohibition on energised work or the conditional exceptions/justifications required by the provision. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate that energised electrical work will only occur under the permitted circumstances.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment except where one of the regulatory exceptions applies, and require documented justification/approval that (a), (b), (c) or (d) is met before energised work proceeds. Include controls/procedures for managing energised work (e.g., decision process, testing under reg 155 where applicable, and verification that no reasonable alternative exists) in addition to competency requirements.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out while equipment is energised (subject to the Division). The policy addresses who may perform electrical work (competency/qualifications) and allows for electrical work to be carried out whether energised or de-energised, without stating a prohibition or control requiring de-energisation. Therefore, the policy does not meet the provision’s requirement to prevent energised electrical work (except where permitted under the Division).
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to require that electrical work must not be performed on energised electrical equipment, except where allowed under the applicable regulatory exceptions (e.g., testing/fault-finding or where de-energising is not reasonably practicable) and only under a documented risk assessment, permit/authorisation process, isolation/LOTO requirements, and specified controls (competent persons, PPE, insulated tools, barriers, supervision). Ensure procedures explicitly place responsibility on the PCBU to prevent energised work outside those exceptions.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy addresses only who may perform electrical work (competency/qualifications) and does not include controls or requirements to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components while energised work is occurring. Therefore, the policy does not satisfy the provision’s risk-prevention obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require controls for energised electrical work that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., isolation where possible; barriers/insulation/shrouding of exposed parts; exclusion zones and access control; use of appropriate PPE and insulated tools; supervision/spotters where required; procedures/permits and pre-job risk assessment; signage and guarding). Assign responsibility to the PCBU and specify implementation and verification requirements.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.
