matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (i.e., a risk management process covering identification, assessment (as required), control, and review). The policy focuses on ensuring electrical work is performed only by competent persons under applicable electrical legislation and notes that training/qualifications affect what work can be performed. While competency controls are relevant to managing electrical risk, the policy does not explicitly require the PCBU to implement a Part 3.1-compliant electrical risk management process (e.g., systematic hazard identification, risk assessment where needed, implementation of controls beyond competency, and ongoing review/monitoring). Therefore it only partially addresses the obligation and is not compliant as written.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require management of electrical risks in accordance with WHS risk management requirements (Part 3.1), including: identifying electrical hazards/risks across design, installation, maintenance and testing; assessing risks where required; implementing and maintaining controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., isolation, RCDs, lockout/tagout, testing, procedures, supervision, PPE) in addition to competency; and reviewing controls after incidents/changes and periodically. Assign responsibilities to the PCBU/management and require documentation/records of risk assessments and control reviews.

source_text:
Electrical workers must complete a practical electrical competency assessment prior to carrying out electrical work if an appropriate electrical licence is not required by their local state/territory regulator. Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory identifies when an electrical competency assessment is required.

The electrical competency assessment evaluates whether an electrical worker has the necessary skills to carry out electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment and can demonstrate safe work practices in line with the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure.

An electrical worker, if applicable, must successfully pass an electrical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO within the past 5 years.

This assessment does not permit electrical workers to carry out any type of electrical work that requires an appropriate electrical licence within their local state or territory.

Refer to Appendix 2 of this guideline for the electrical competency assessment template that must be used.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes a requirement for electrical workers to complete a practical electrical competency assessment (within the past 5 years) when a local electrical licence is not required, focused on work on non-fixed electrical equipment and safe work practices. The PROVISION imposes specific duties on a PCBU for electrical work on energised electrical equipment: it must be carried out by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and with a qualified safety observer (unless an exception applies), plus ensuring proper use of tools/PPE and requiring the safety observer to have been assessed within the previous 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. The POLICY does not address energised electrical work controls (SWMS, safety observer, tool/PPE testing/maintenance, 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment), and its 5-year assessment interval is not aligned to the PROVISION’s 12-month requirement for safety observer rescue/resuscitation competence. Therefore, while related (both concern electrical work competence/safety), the POLICY does not cover key mandatory elements of the PROVISION and is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/guideline to explicitly require, for energised electrical work: (1) work is performed by a competent person with suitable, properly tested, and well-maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) a SWMS is prepared and followed meeting the specified content requirements; (3) a competent safety observer is present unless the testing-only + risk assessment no-serious-risk exception applies; (4) safety observers are assessed at least every 12 months as competent in rescue and resuscitation; and (5) the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE. Also clarify scope/interaction between the 5-year competency assessment and any additional competency requirements for energised work.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires specific preconditions before energised electrical work commences: a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person, ensuring the work area and isolation/disconnection point are clear/labelled/operable, and formal authorisation after consultation with workplace management/control. The policy instead focuses on requiring an electrical competency assessment (where a licence is not required) and its validity period/template. It does not address energised electrical work controls, does not require a risk assessment (recorded or otherwise), does not specify access/egress and isolation point requirements, and does not require authorisation/consultation prior to commencing energised work. Therefore, the policy does not fulfill the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a risk assessment for the proposed energised work, conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or document applicability of the stated exception); and (4) formal authorisation to proceed after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include roles, documentation templates/records, and a pre-start checklist to evidence compliance.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded, which determines when licensing/competency controls are applicable. The policy establishes an internal control requiring a practical electrical competency assessment (within the past 5 years) before an electrical worker performs electrical work where a local licence is not required, and it explicitly states the assessment does not authorise work that requires a licence. This aligns with the provision’s framework by applying competency requirements to electrical work (as defined) while not attempting to override jurisdictional licensing requirements or expand authorisation beyond what regulators permit. The policy is not inconsistent with the provision’s exclusions; rather, it sets a stricter internal requirement for certain work even where licensing may not be mandated.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The policy instead focuses on worker competency assessments (and licensing boundaries) and does not state any requirement to test equipment for energisation prior to work, does not require that testing be performed by a competent person for that purpose, and does not include the exposed-parts treated-as-energised and HV earthing requirements. Therefore, the policy does not fulfill the specific operational safety obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/guideline to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment, the equipment must be tested for energisation by a competent person (in accordance with the applicable testing method requirements), require that exposed parts be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and require that high-voltage exposed parts be earthed after being de-energised. Define/align 'competent person' and reference the relevant procedures/regulations for how testing and isolation are to be performed.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses electrical worker competency assessments (skills/authorisation to perform certain electrical work on non-fixed equipment when a licence is not required). The PROVISION imposes a duty on the business/undertaking to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and to keep specified testing records. While both concern electrical safety and competence, the POLICY does not establish (or evidence) a program for regular inspection/testing of equipment under specified harsh conditions, does not require keeping testing records until next test/removal, and does not specify record contents (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) or tagging. Therefore, the POLICY does not fulfill the PROVISION’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add requirements to the policy/procedure to: (1) identify in-scope equipment (plug-in equipment used in environments likely to cause damage: moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust); (2) mandate regular inspection and testing intervals based on risk/conditions; (3) ensure testing is performed by a 'competent person' (define competence for testing separately from the 5-year worker competency assessment, if needed); (4) require pre-use inspection for new/unused equipment for obvious damage; (5) maintain testing records until next test or disposal/removal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next due date; and (6) allow/use tagging as a record mechanism.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

matches.target_title: 114 Accredited assessor must act in accordance with accreditation
matches.target_text:
(1) An accredited assessor must not conduct a competency assessment unless: 

(a) the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited; and   
(b) the accredited assessor conducts the competency assessment for or on behalf of an RTO. 

(2) An accredited assessor must not issue a notice of satisfactory assessment unless the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited. 

(3) An accredited assessor who conducts a competency assessment must do so in accordance with the conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(4) An accredited assessor who issues a notice of satisfactory assessment must do so in accordance with any conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(5) Subregulations (1) to (4) do not apply if the regulator is the accredited assessor. 

Note 

See section 43 of the Act.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be performed by a competent person and, where a detailed inspection includes an electrical installation, the inspector must be qualified (or assisted by someone qualified) to inspect electrical installations. The policy instead focuses on a CSIRO practical electrical competency assessment for electrical workers performing electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment in jurisdictions where an electrical licence is not required. It explicitly states the assessment does not permit work requiring an electrical licence. The policy does not address (a) amusement devices/passenger ropeways, (b) maintenance/inspection/testing obligations, (c) ensuring work is done in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a maintenance manual, or (d) the specific requirement that detailed inspections involving electrical installations be performed by a qualified electrical installation inspector (or assisted by one). Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s competency/qualification and procedural requirements for these devices.
compliance.remediation: Update or add a procedure for amusement devices/passenger ropeways that: (1) mandates maintenance/inspection/testing be performed by a competent person; (2) requires compliance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; (3) requires that any detailed inspection including an electrical installation be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations or performed with documented assistance from such a qualified person; and (4) clarifies that internal competency assessments do not substitute for legally required electrical installation inspection qualifications/licences.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy establishes a control measure to manage electrical risk by requiring electrical workers (where a licence is not required) to complete and pass a practical electrical competency assessment, demonstrate safe work practices aligned with the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure, and maintain currency within the past 5 years. It also clarifies that the assessment does not substitute for legally required electrical licensing, supporting compliance with regulatory expectations. While the provision is broader than competency assessment alone, the policy content is consistent with and supports the obligation to manage electrical risks.

source_text:
The verification of electrical competency must establish whether the electrical worker:

1. Has the licences, qualifications, training and experience necessary to safely carry out the required electrical work; and   
2. Can demonstrate an understanding of the specific risks and control measures needed to carry out specific electrical work tasks.

What to consider when completing a verification form?

Prior to carrying out electrical work in CSIRO, an electrical worker must complete and submit a verification of electrical competency form to their line manager for consideration and approval.

When completing the form consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, if applicable, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment and SWIs need to be attached to the verification form.

What to consider when reviewing and approving a verification form?

The Line Manager along with the Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) should review the details in Part 2 & 3 of a submitted Verification of electrical competency.

When completing Part 4 & 5, discuss the electrical tasks to be performed and consider the following:

a. Does the licence/qualification/training/experience address the work described in Part 2? i.e. Do they require an electrical licence, additional training, equipment/tools, supervision?   
b. Any of the electrical work tasks need to be performed energised – an application to undertake energised electrical work needs to be completed and submitted by the electrical worker/competent person to obtain permission to work energised.   
c. Was there a previous electrical approval – is this a renewal or has there been a change in the electrical tasks?   
d. Has the worker provided copies of licences, certificates, qualification – copies must be provided. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must be ceased until all supporting documentation provided.   
e. Are the licences, certificates, qualifications still in date and valid e.g. some licences have a review/renewal date. If out of date, the worker should be notified, and it noted on the verification form.   
f. Have all known risks and hazards been assessed for the work to be undertaken, has it been documented and have the control measures been implemented - copies of Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions must be provided for the electrical tasks. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must cease until the completed risk assessments and SWI, if applicable, have been provided.   
g. Are there further requirements for the worker to be approved - where it has been identified that additional training, supervision, procedures, etc are required, it is to be noted on the workers verification form and actioned appropriately.

Setting conditions and limitations to electrical work

Upon reviewing and approving verification form, a Line Manager may want to set conditions or limitation to what electrical work an electrical worker can carry out based on the risk and competency of the work.

Examples of conditions and limitations include:

 Electrical work is restricted to testing only, (no working on energised circuits).   
 Working on energised circuits strictly in accordance with the conditions of an electrical licence.  Working strictly under supervision by an electrically competent and qualified electrical worker. Isolate, tag, de- energise using appropriate PPE and work on de-energised equipment or installation.   
Restricted to electrical measurements on live external terminals, with appropriate PPE. Replacement of components in accordance with engineering specifications, like-for-like on deenergised circuits.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related because it governs verification/approval of electrical competency and conditions for energised electrical work, which is directly relevant to the PROVISION’s requirements for energised electrical work (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS content, and safety observer requirements/exception). However, the POLICY does not fully implement the PROVISION’s specific mandatory controls for energised work: (1) It requires competency verification and mentions PPE and an application/permission process for energised work, but it does not explicitly require that tools/testing equipment/PPE used for energised work are suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order, nor that the PCBU ensures (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use of such equipment. (2) It requires Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, but does not explicitly require a Safe Work Method Statement for energised electrical work, nor does it specify the required SWMS elements (identify work; hazards/risks; control measures; implementation/monitoring/review). (3) It does not require a safety observer for energised work, does not specify observer competency requirements, and does not include the 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment requirement or the limited testing + risk assessment exception. Therefore, the POLICY only partially aligns and is not compliant with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY and associated forms/processes to explicitly require, for energised electrical work: (a) work is performed by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; and the PCBU ensures (so far as reasonably practicable) proper use; (b) a SWMS is prepared and used, with mandatory content matching the regulation (work identification; hazards/risks; controls; implementation/monitoring/review); (c) a safety observer is present unless the testing-only + risk assessment shows no serious risk, and require the observer to be competent and assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate; (d) incorporate checks/evidence fields in the verification/approval workflow for SWMS, equipment test/maintenance status, PPE/tool suitability, and safety observer qualifications/recency.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it governs competency verification, risk assessment documentation, and approval/permission processes for electrical work, including energised work. However, the PROVISION imposes specific preconditions that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences: (1)(a) a risk assessment conducted; (2) that risk assessment must be conducted by a competent person and recorded; (1)(b) work area clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) isolation/disconnection point clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly; and (1)(d) authorisation after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. The POLICY partially addresses (1)(a)/(2) by requiring Activity Risk Assessments/SWIs to be completed, documented, and provided, and by requiring approval/permission for energised work. It also partially addresses authorisation via line manager/ESO review and approval. However, it does not explicitly require that the energised-work risk assessment be conducted by a competent person (as distinct from the worker) and explicitly recorded for the energised task, nor does it include the specific site-condition checks required by (1)(b) and (1)(c) (clear access/egress, labelled/clear/quickly operable isolation point) as mandatory pre-start conditions for energised work. It also does not explicitly require authorisation to occur after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace (it implies line manager involvement but does not state the consultation requirement). Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s specific preconditions for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure (and verification/energised-work application forms) to explicitly require, before energised work starts: (1) a task-specific risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) confirmation the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) confirmation the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly (and note the exception where applicable); and (4) documented authorisation granted only after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Add checklist fields and evidence/recordkeeping requirements for these items.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is about verifying an electrical worker’s competency (licences, training, experience, risk understanding) and managerial approval/conditions for electrical work, including requirements around energised work permissions and provision of risk assessments/SWIs. The PROVISION imposes a specific operational duty on the PCBU: before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and high-voltage exposed parts must be earthed after de-energising. While the POLICY discusses planning for de-energised vs energised work and risk assessments, it does not explicitly require pre-work testing to confirm de-energised status, does not mandate treating exposed parts as energised until isolation and test confirms otherwise, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully implement the specific controls required by the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (and associated verification form/SWI requirements) to explicitly require: (1) testing by a competent person before electrical work to determine whether equipment is energised; (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised by test; and (3) for high-voltage work, earthing of exposed parts after de-energising. Cross-reference the required testing method/procedure (e.g., applicable internal procedure aligned to regulatory testing requirements) and ensure these steps are embedded in mandatory SWIs/ARAs and manager approval checks.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes an internal process for verifying electrical competency (licences/qualifications/training/experience, risk understanding, manager/ESO review, documentation, and conditions/limitations including controls for energised work). The PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and enumerates specific exclusions (tasks that are not electrical work) and conditions under which certain excluded activities may occur (e.g., supervised work on ducts/conduits when wiring is not energised; assisting a licensed worker under direct supervision with no contact with energised equipment; eligibility work other than on energised equipment). The POLICY does not define “electrical work” or explicitly incorporate the PROVISION’s scope and exclusions. As written, the POLICY applies broadly to “electrical work in CSIRO” and could be interpreted to require verification/approval for activities that the PROVISION explicitly excludes from “electrical work” (e.g., plugging in equipment, replacing a light bulb/fuse, non-electrical component work with no electrical risk). Conversely, the POLICY also does not explicitly address the PROVISION’s conditional exclusions (direct supervision/no contact with energised equipment for assistants; supervision requirements for certain conduit/duct work; eligibility work limitations), meaning the internal controls are not clearly aligned to the regulatory boundary of what is and is not “electrical work” and the conditions attached to excluded activities. Therefore, while the POLICY supports safe practice, it is not compliant as a mapping to the PROVISION because it fails to reflect the statutory definition and exclusions that determine when the policy process must apply and what supervision/energisation constraints apply to certain activities.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to (1) adopt or reference the jurisdictional definition of “electrical work” and explicitly list (or reference) the exclusions in the PROVISION; (2) clarify when the verification/approval process is required versus when tasks fall outside “electrical work” (while still allowing separate risk-based controls for non-electrical-work tasks if desired); (3) explicitly incorporate the PROVISION’s conditional requirements for excluded activities (e.g., direct supervision and no contact with energised equipment for assistants; supervision and de-energised wiring conditions for ducts/conduits/troughs work; eligibility work limited to non-energised equipment); and (4) ensure the energised-work permission process is clearly limited to circumstances where energised electrical work is legally permitted and performed by appropriately licensed/authorised persons.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1). The POLICY establishes a structured process to manage electrical risks by verifying electrical worker competency (licences/qualifications/training/experience and understanding of task-specific risks and controls), requiring risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions, requiring managerial and Electrical Safety Officer review/approval, prohibiting work where supporting documentation is missing, addressing validity/renewal of licences, and setting conditions/limitations (including controls for energised work and supervision). These measures are consistent with a risk management approach for electrical risks across maintenance/testing and related activities referenced in the PROVISION.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the specified necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, work requires energisation to be done properly, testing required under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy establishes a competency verification and approval process and requires an application/permission process for energised work, but it does not explicitly incorporate the provision’s threshold test (i.e., requiring documented justification that one of the legal necessity grounds applies before energised work is permitted). It also frames energised work permission primarily around holding an appropriate licence and obtaining internal permission, which is not sufficient to meet the regulatory precondition that energised work must be avoided unless necessary under the listed criteria.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy and associated forms/approval workflow to explicitly require and document that energised work is only approved when at least one of the provision’s conditions is met (health/safety necessity; necessary for proper work; testing required under reg 155; or no reasonable alternative). Add mandatory fields/checklist for the specific justification category, evidence of why de-energised work is not reasonably practicable, and manager/ESO sign-off confirming the legal criterion is satisfied before permission is granted.

matches.target_title: 252 Who can be the design verifier
matches.target_text:
(1) A person is eligible to be a design verifier for the design of an item of plant if the person is a competent person.

(2) Despite subregulation (1), a person is not eligible to be a design verifier for the design of an item of plant if the person was involved in the production of the design.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

source_text:
Section 154 of the WHS Regulations states that electrical work is not to be carried out on energised electrical equipment. However, exemptions do apply in certain circumstances provided in Section 157 of the regulations. Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for more information about energised electrical work requirements.

Wherever possible electrical work must be carried out de-energised, this includes testing and fault finding. All possible de-energised working methods must be identified and tried before working energised should be considered.

‘Test for Dead’ before you touch

Before electrical work is carried out, the equipment needs to be tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. You need to ensure:

 Each exposed part of the equipment is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
 Each high voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised.

Electrical equipment that is de-energised must not be inadvertently energised while work is being carried out.

Work on Electrical Installations

Electrical work on an installation involving any part thereof, in service following construction, alteration or repair, it shall be verified, as far as practicable, that the installation is safe to energise and will operate in accordance with the requirements of AS 3000. Precautions shall be taken to ensure the safety of persons and to avoid damage to property and the electrical installation equipment during inspecting and testing.

Where electrical installation is an alteration or repair to an existing electrical installation, it shall be verified that the alteration or repair complies with this AS 3000 and does not impair the safety of the existing electrical installation.

matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.
