matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets internal requirements for when energised electrical work may occur and emphasizes de-energised work as the default, aligning with the regulatory prohibition and exceptions. The POLICY states energised work is generally prohibited (refers to s154) and that all possible de-energised methods must be identified and tried before considering energised work, which is consistent with the PROVISION’s requirement that energised work must not be carried out unless one of the necessity/no-reasonable-alternative/testing conditions applies. The POLICY also includes controls such as 'test for dead', treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, earthing high-voltage parts after de-energising, and preventing inadvertent re-energisation—measures that support safe execution consistent with the regulation’s intent. While the POLICY does not restate each exception in the same wording as (1)(a)-(d), it references the exemptions (s157) and establishes a stricter default (de-energise wherever possible), which does not conflict with the PROVISION.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses working de-energised wherever possible, “test for dead,” treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, earthing high-voltage parts after de-energising, preventing inadvertent re-energisation, and verifying installations are safe to energise per AS 3000. The PROVISION, however, sets specific mandatory prerequisites before energised electrical work commences: (1) a risk assessment conducted, (2) the work area clear of obstructions for access/exit, (3) the isolation/disconnection point clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable, and (4) formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace; additionally, the risk assessment must be conducted by a competent person and recorded. The POLICY does not explicitly require a recorded risk assessment for energised work, does not require the specific access/egress and isolation-point marking/quick-operability controls, and does not require authorisation after consultation. Therefore it does not fully meet the PROVISION’s prerequisites for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (and associated procedures/forms) to explicitly require, before any energised electrical work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to ensure easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly (and address the stated exception where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Implement an authorisation/permit and risk-assessment recordkeeping process to evidence compliance.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses working de-energised wherever possible, “test for dead”, treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, earthing high voltage parts after de-energising, preventing inadvertent re-energisation, and verifying installations are safe to energise per AS 3000. These topics are functionally related to the PROVISION, which sets mandatory conditions for when energised electrical work is carried out (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, safe work method statement (SWMS) content requirements, and a safety observer unless an exception applies). However, the POLICY does not include several required controls for energised work: it does not require that energised work be carried out in accordance with a SWMS, does not specify SWMS minimum content (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review), does not require a safety observer for energised work (nor define competence and annual rescue/resuscitation assessment), and does not require ensuring (so far as reasonably practicable) that tools/testing equipment/PPE are used properly. Therefore, the POLICY is not compliant with the PROVISION’s explicit requirements for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add an energised electrical work procedure) to explicitly require: (1) energised electrical work only by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) a SWMS for all energised electrical work, including the minimum content in subregulation (3); (3) a safety observer for energised work, with defined competence and evidence of rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies; (4) a requirement that the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE; and (5) document the risk assessment process and criteria for when the safety observer exception can be used.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY sets requirements for how “electrical work” must be performed (primarily de-energised, test-before-touch, prevent inadvertent re-energisation, verification to AS 3000), while the PROVISION defines what activities are and are not “electrical work.” A compliance check is necessary to ensure the POLICY’s scope aligns with the statutory definition and exclusions. The POLICY does not define “electrical work” or acknowledge key exclusions in the PROVISION (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work with no electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs where safely performed by non-experts, certain supervised assistance, manufacturing processes, and conduit/duct work under conditions). As written, the POLICY could be interpreted as applying its electrical-work controls to tasks that the PROVISION explicitly says are not electrical work, creating overreach and potential confusion about when the policy applies and who must comply (e.g., non-electrical workers changing a light bulb). While being more conservative is not inherently unlawful, the lack of scope alignment and clarity means the POLICY does not demonstrably implement the PROVISION’s definitional boundaries and could lead to incorrect classification and procedural requirements.
compliance.remediation: Add a clear definition section that adopts the PROVISION’s definition of “electrical work” and explicitly lists/links the exclusions. State the POLICY’s scope: (1) which requirements apply only to “electrical work” as defined; (2) which, if any, requirements also apply to excluded tasks as a separate organisational safety standard (and why). Include role/competency and supervision rules consistent with the exclusions (e.g., supervised assistants, non-expert replacements) and provide examples to prevent misclassification.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses safe methods for performing electrical work (e.g., working de-energised, “test for dead,” preventing inadvertent re-energisation, and verification/testing of installations to AS 3000 after construction/alteration/repair). The PROVISION, however, imposes a separate and specific duty on the PCBU to ensure certain plug-in electrical equipment used in damaging environments is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, with exemptions for new/unused equipment (inspection for obvious damage), and requires keeping specified testing records until next test or disposal. The POLICY does not establish a program for regular inspection/testing of in-scope equipment, does not state the environmental trigger conditions, does not include the new/unused equipment inspection requirement, and does not require maintaining records with the mandated fields (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) or tagging. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add a section requiring regular inspection and testing by a competent person for plug-in electrical equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals, dust), define inspection/testing intervals or a risk-based schedule, require pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment, and implement recordkeeping that captures tester name, test date, outcome, and next due date (e.g., via tags and/or a register) retained until the next test or disposal/removal.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out on energised electrical equipment. The POLICY explicitly states that electrical work is not to be carried out on energised equipment, requires de-energised methods wherever possible (including testing and fault finding), and includes controls such as 'test for dead' and preventing inadvertent re-energisation. These statements align with and operationalize the prohibition in the PROVISION (while noting exemptions exist elsewhere).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty: if electrical work is carried out on energised electrical equipment, the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy strongly emphasises de-energised work, “test for dead,” treating exposed parts as energised until isolated, earthing high voltage parts after de-energising, and preventing inadvertent re-energisation. However, it does not state the required control for the scenario where energised work is actually performed—i.e., preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., barriers, exclusion zones, insulating covers, access control, supervision). Therefore, the policy does not fully address the provision’s obligation for energised work conditions.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements for energised electrical work controls, including: establishing exclusion zones and access control; using physical barriers/insulating shrouds and covers over exposed energised parts; ensuring only authorised/competent persons are present; implementing a documented energised work permit/JSA with specific controls; using appropriate PPE and insulated tools; and supervision/spotter arrangements where needed to prevent inadvertent contact.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated from supply and, once isolated, is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or else replaced/permanently removed. The policy focuses on prohibiting energized work, requiring de-energised work where possible, 'test for dead', treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, earthing high voltage parts after de-energising, preventing inadvertent re-energisation during work, and verifying installations are safe to energise after construction/alteration/repair. However, it does not explicitly address the specific obligation to manage 'unsafe electrical equipment' by (1) disconnecting/isolating it upon being unsafe, and (2) prohibiting reconnection until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. The policy’s testing/verification language is related but does not clearly cover the required controls for unsafe equipment status and reconnection prohibition.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit section on unsafe electrical equipment requiring: (1) immediate disconnection/isolation when there are reasonable grounds to believe equipment is unsafe; (2) tagging/lockout and controls to prevent reconnection; (3) prohibition on reconnection until the equipment is repaired or tested and confirmed safe by a competent person; and (4) replacement or permanent removal from service if not repaired/tested safe. Define 'unsafe' consistent with the regulation (reasonable grounds) and include responsibilities, records, and escalation steps.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out. The POLICY explicitly states: "Electrical equipment that is de-energised must not be inadvertently energised while work is being carried out." This directly addresses the same obligation and indicates the required control/outcome is mandated by the policy.

source_text:
All electrical tools and equipment should be suitable for the work and be maintained in good working order, including regular maintenance, inspection and testing. Inadequate maintenance may lead to serious electrical risks, for example an insulating medium might conceal a mechanical defect that could cause an open circuit in a testing device. Where any doubt exists about adequacy of the insulation of tools and equipment they should not be used and removed from service.

Insulating barriers and insulating mats

Insulating covers and mats used for electrical purposes should comply with AS/NZS2978: Insulating mats for electrical purposes. Insulated barriers should be of suitable material to effectively separate electrical workers from adjacent energised electrical equipment or energised parts at a higher voltage e.g. protection from low voltage energised parts when working with extra-low voltage. These items should be visually inspected before and after each use. If the items have a defect that adversely affect the performance, then it must be removed from service.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses maintenance, inspection and testing of electrical tools/equipment and removal from service if insulation is doubtful, which is functionally related to the PROVISION requiring regular inspection and testing by a competent person for certain socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments, plus recordkeeping of testing. However, the POLICY does not explicitly require that (1) the inspection/testing be performed by a competent person, (2) the requirement applies specifically to socket-outlet supplied equipment used in specified harsh operating conditions, (3) new/unused equipment be inspected for obvious damage before first use (and exemption from regular testing until used), and (4) records of testing be kept with the required minimum contents (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) and retained until next test or disposal. The POLICY’s visual inspection requirement for insulating mats/barriers is related but does not satisfy the PROVISION’s testing/recordkeeping obligations for electrical equipment.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to: (1) mandate regular inspection and electrical testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals, dust); (2) require new/unused equipment to be inspected for obvious damage before first use; (3) require that unsafe equipment is not used and is removed from service; (4) implement a testing record system retained until the next test or disposal, capturing tester name, test date, outcome, and next due date (e.g., tag plus central register).

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The policy focuses on suitability and maintenance of electrical tools/equipment and inspection/removal from service of insulating mats/barriers, but it does not require pre-work energisation testing by a competent person, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised' rule, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore it does not meet the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) before any electrical work on electrical equipment, testing by a competent person to confirm whether the equipment is energised (with reference to the applicable testing method/procedure); (2) all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) each high-voltage exposed part must be earthed after being de-energised. Include roles/competency requirements, documentation/records of testing, and integration with existing inspection/maintenance controls.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it addresses suitability, inspection/testing, and maintenance of electrical tools/equipment and insulating barriers/mats used for electrical purposes, which aligns with the PROVISION’s requirement that energised electrical work be carried out by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained. However, the POLICY does not cover several mandatory elements of the PROVISION for energised electrical work: (1) it does not require that energised electrical work be carried out by a competent person; (2) it does not require work to be performed in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) nor specify SWMS content; (3) it does not require a safety observer (or define the exception conditions), nor the observer’s competence and annual rescue/resuscitation assessment; and (4) it does not explicitly require, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. While the POLICY includes inspection/maintenance expectations (and an AS/NZS standard for insulating mats), it is incomplete against the full set of obligations in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add a linked procedure) for energised electrical work to: (1) require energised electrical work be performed only by a competent person; (2) mandate a SWMS for energised electrical work and include the required SWMS elements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review); (3) require a safety observer for energised electrical work, define observer competence and the requirement for rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the previous 12 months, and document the limited exception (testing-only plus risk assessment showing no serious risk); and (4) require, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly (with supervision/training/verification as needed).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical tools/equipment to be maintained, inspected and tested, and explicitly states that where there is doubt about insulation adequacy (or defects in insulating mats/barriers), the item must not be used and must be removed from service. The PROVISION requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/permanently removed. The POLICY’s “do not use/remove from service” control aligns with the PROVISION’s requirement to prevent continued use and reconnection of unsafe equipment, and the POLICY’s inspection/testing expectations support the ‘tested and found safe’ condition before reuse. While the POLICY does not explicitly state “disconnect/isolate from electricity supply,” its removal-from-service requirement functionally satisfies the intent to prevent energization and use of unsafe equipment.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (including risks arising from maintenance and testing of electrical equipment) in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy sets controls to manage electrical risks by requiring electrical tools/equipment to be suitable for the work, maintained in good working order with regular maintenance/inspection/testing, removed from service where insulation adequacy is in doubt, and requiring insulating mats/barriers to meet a specified standard (AS/NZS2978), be suitable for separation from energised parts, be visually inspected before/after use, and be removed from service if defective. These measures are consistent with risk management expectations for electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that electrical equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested. The policy discusses maintaining tools/equipment in good working order and mentions regular maintenance, inspection and testing, and removing items from service where insulation adequacy is in doubt or defects are found. However, it does not explicitly require that equipment subject to mandatory testing under regulation 150 must not be used if it has not been tested, nor does it reference the regulatory testing requirement or establish a clear control to prevent use pending required testing. Therefore, the policy does not fully satisfy the specific prohibition in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement that any electrical equipment required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested (and is within the required test interval), including a process/control for tagging, recording test status, and removing/locking out untested equipment from service.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, persons are prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The policy establishes controls that support this outcome by requiring electrical tools/equipment to be suitable and maintained, removing doubtful/defective insulated tools/equipment from service, and specifying compliant insulating mats/barriers (AS/NZS2978), effective separation from energised parts, and mandatory visual inspection before/after use with removal from service if defects affect performance. These measures functionally address preventing inadvertent contact and associated electrical risk during energised work.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing is carried out by a competent person and in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also requires that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation be performed by a qualified person (or assisted by one). The policy addresses electrical tools/equipment and insulating mats/barriers, requiring suitability, regular maintenance/inspection/testing, and removal from service if insulation adequacy is in doubt or defects are found. However, it does not specify that maintenance/inspection/testing for amusement devices/ropeways must be performed by a competent person, does not require adherence to manufacturer/designer recommendations or a formal maintenance manual, and does not address the qualification requirement for inspections involving electrical installations. Therefore, it does not fully meet the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for amusement devices and passenger ropeways to: (1) mandate maintenance, inspection and testing (as necessary) be performed by a competent person; (2) require work be conducted in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; (3) require that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation be performed by a qualified electrical inspector or by a competent person assisted by a qualified person; and (4) document competency/qualification verification and retain maintenance/inspection/testing records.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

source_text:
If an electrical worker needs to carry out electrical work on a site other than their home site, they should notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) for the site they plan to work on of their intention to conduct electrical work.

Upon request, electrical workers should provide the ESO with a copy of their completed Verification of electrical competency, Activity risk assessment and SWI for review.

Example – Notifying a Site ESO when working on a different site

An Electrical worker from Bribie Island needs to go to St Lucia to carry out electrical testing of instrumentation. If there is a Site ESO at St Lucia, the worker should the Site ESO of their plans to conduct electrical work.

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person.

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” (and exclusions). The policy sets a procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) when conducting electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. This is functionally related because the policy is triggered by the same activity category defined in the provision (“electrical work”). However, the policy does not incorporate or reference the provision’s definition/exclusions, so it may be applied to tasks that are not “electrical work” under the provision (e.g., plugging equipment into a socket, replacing a light bulb/fuse, or other excluded activities). This creates a mismatch between the scope of the policy trigger and the legally/technically defined scope of “electrical work,” making the policy not fully aligned with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly align its trigger with the provision’s definition of “electrical work,” including stating that notification is required when performing “electrical work” as defined in the applicable legislation/standard and clarifying that excluded activities (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, replacing light bulbs/fuses where safely performed by non-experts, non-electrical component work without electrical risk) are not within scope unless otherwise required by site rules. Optionally add examples distinguishing included vs excluded tasks.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a broad duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (in accordance with Part 3.1). The policy addresses a narrow administrative control: notifying the Site Electrical Safety Officer when working at a non-home site and providing competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. While this can support risk management, it does not explicitly require or describe managing electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, implementation of controls, maintenance/testing controls, review of control effectiveness) nor does it clearly assign the duty to the PCBU. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the broader regulatory obligation.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy (or reference a linked procedure) to explicitly require electrical risk management in line with Part 3.1, including: identifying electrical hazards at each site; assessing risks; implementing and documenting controls using the hierarchy of controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, testing and tagging, RCDs, exclusion zones, permits); ensuring safe design/installation/maintenance/testing processes; defining PCBU responsibilities and oversight; requiring pre-start/site-specific risk assessment and authorization before work; and periodic review/audit of controls and incident reporting.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

source_text: To ensure that electrical work on de-energised equipment cannot be inadvertently re-energised, workers are required to isolate the equipment at the electrical supply source. The switch must be locked out and tagged to prevent re-energisation. Workers must test the electrical equipment to confirm that it has been de-energised before commencing work. The worker should use personal locks and danger tags at the isolation point to prevent the risk of inadvertent re-energisation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The policy is functionally related because it addresses controls required before working on de-energised electrical equipment (isolation/lockout/tagout and testing). It complies with Provision (1) in substance by requiring testing to confirm equipment is de-energised before commencing work, but it does not explicitly require that the testing be performed by a "competent person". It also does not address Provision (2)(a) that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised, nor Provision (2)(b) that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised. Therefore, the policy does not fully meet the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) explicitly require testing to be performed by a competent person (and reference applicable testing procedure requirements); (2) state that all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) require that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation, including specifying when/where this applies and the responsible role.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during electrical work. The policy directly addresses this obligation by requiring isolation at the supply source, lockout and tagging, use of personal locks and danger tags, and verification testing before commencing work—controls that are designed to prevent inadvertent re-energisation while work is carried out.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses isolation/lockout-tagout and verification of de-energisation to prevent inadvertent re-energisation during work on de-energised equipment. The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and, once isolated, not be reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found safe, or else replaced/permanently removed. While the policy covers disconnection/isolation and preventing re-energisation, it does not state the required conditions for reconnection (repair or test and found safe) nor the alternative requirement to replace or permanently remove unsafe equipment from use. It also does not explicitly scope the requirement to 'unsafe electrical equipment' as defined by reasonable grounds.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment is isolated/disconnected and must not be reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and confirmed safe, or alternatively replaced or permanently removed from use. Include a clear criterion/process for determining when equipment is considered 'unsafe' (e.g., reasonable grounds) and document controls for preventing reconnection until the required conditions are met.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision regulates when electrical work on energised equipment may occur (it must not be carried out unless one of the listed exceptions applies). The policy addresses de-energised work controls (isolation, lockout/tagout, and test-for-dead) but does not state the core prohibition on energised electrical work or the limited circumstances under which energised work is permitted. Because the policy is silent on restricting/authorising energised work to only the provision’s exceptions, it does not demonstrate compliance with the requirement to ensure energised work is not carried out unless the specified conditions are met.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment except where one of the permitted conditions applies (health/safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Include a requirement for documented justification/approval for energised work against these criteria and clarify that any energised testing is only allowed within those permitted circumstances.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out while equipment is energised. The policy establishes controls to achieve de-energised work: isolating at the supply source, locking out and tagging to prevent re-energisation, and testing to confirm de-energisation before commencing work. These measures functionally ensure work is performed only when equipment is not energised, aligning with the provision’s requirement.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_text: Isolation points must be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent the electrical equipment from being inadvertently re-energised. The control mechanism should require a deliberate action to engage or dis-engage the device. It must withstand conditions that could lead to the isolation failing. Examples of this include switches with a built-in lock, and lock out for switches, circuit breakers, fuses and safety lockout jaws ‘hasps’.

matches.target_title: 210 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that any operator's controls are:

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and 

(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the person with management or control of the plant at a workplace must ensure that the operator's controls:

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and 

(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, either: 

(i) cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; or 

(ii) if subparagraph (i) cannot be complied with because the plant must be operated by a person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant, cannot be operated except by a person authorised by the person with management or control of the plant for that purpose; and 

(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: 

(i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and be robust (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION requires operator controls to be located/guarded to prevent unintentional activation and to be able to be locked into the "off" position to enable disconnection of motive power. These requirements are functionally aligned: both aim to prevent unintended energisation/activation and require lockable controls for isolation. While the POLICY additionally mentions robustness/withstanding conditions leading to isolation failure, the PROVISION’s lock-off and prevention of unintentional activation satisfy the core functional intent of preventing inadvertent re-energisation via deliberate, lockable isolation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses controls at isolation points to prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices requiring deliberate action and able to withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure). The PROVISION requires, before energised electrical work commences, that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/egress, and capable of being operated quickly, along with risk assessment, recording, and authorisation/consultation steps. While both concern isolation points for electrical work, the POLICY does not cover several mandatory preconditions in the PROVISION (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; ensuring the work area is clear; ensuring the isolation point is clearly marked/labelled; ensuring it is capable of being operated quickly; and formal authorisation after consultation). Therefore, the POLICY is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) a competent-person risk assessment before energised electrical work, with the assessment recorded; (2) the work area to be cleared of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point to be clearly marked/labelled and kept unobstructed for access/exit; (4) confirmation that the isolation/disconnection point can be operated quickly; and (5) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Include the exception conditions where subregulation (1)(c) does not apply.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both address controls that prevent inadvertent re-energisation/restart of electrical plant through deliberate-action mechanisms and robustness against failure. The PROVISION requires emergency stop controls (where fitted, and especially where multiple are fitted) to be of the “stop and lock-off” type so the plant cannot be restarted until the specific emergency stop is reset, and that the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical/electronic malfunction. The POLICY focuses on isolation points needing control mechanisms (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices) to prevent inadvertent re-energisation and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure. However, the POLICY does not explicitly require emergency stop controls to be stop-and-lock-off (reset-required) nor does it address prominence/marking/accessibility, red coloration, or immunity to electrical/electronic circuit malfunction. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s specific emergency-stop requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a specific emergency-stop section) to require: (1) where multiple emergency stops exist, they must be “stop and lock-off” such that restart is impossible until the actuated emergency stop is reset; (2) emergency stop controls are prominent, clearly/durably marked, and immediately accessible to each operator; (3) associated actuators (handle/bar/push button) are red; and (4) emergency stop function is fail-safe/not adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction. Ensure procedures and design standards reflect these requirements and verify through inspection/testing.

matches.target_title: 191 Emergency stop controls
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for the multiple emergency stop controls to be of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the design of the plant includes an emergency stop control for the plant, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides: 

(a) for the stop control to be prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) for any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control to be coloured red; and   
(c) that the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both address safety controls intended to prevent unintended re-energisation/restart of equipment after a stop/isolation action. The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energising, require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION requires emergency stop controls (where fitted, and especially where multiple are fitted) to be of the stop-and-lock-off type so the plant cannot be restarted unless the used emergency stop is reset, and that the stop control is prominent/marked/accessible, red, and not adversely affected by electrical/electronic malfunction. While the PROVISION aligns with the POLICY on preventing inadvertent restart and robustness against malfunction, it does not address isolation points or lockout mechanisms at isolation points (e.g., lockable isolators, lockout hasps) and does not explicitly require a deliberate action to engage/disengage an isolation device. Therefore, the PROVISION alone does not fully satisfy the POLICY requirements for isolation points.
compliance.remediation: Add/verify design and procedures for isolation points that include lockable isolation devices or lockout provisions (e.g., built-in lockable isolator switches, lockout for breakers/fuses, safety lockout hasps) requiring deliberate action to operate, and ensure the isolation mechanism is robust against foreseeable conditions that could cause isolation failure. Document these features separately from emergency stop controls.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.
