compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices) and that require deliberate action and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure. The PROVISION requires testing to confirm equipment is not energised before work, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. While both address electrical safety around de-energisation/isolation, the PROVISION does not require (or describe) lockable/deliberate-action control mechanisms at isolation points to prevent inadvertent re-energisation. Therefore, the POLICY obligation is not satisfied by the PROVISION text alone.
compliance.remediation: Add/ensure requirements and controls for lockout/tagout or equivalent deliberate-action, lockable isolation mechanisms at isolation points (e.g., lockable isolators, lockout devices for breakers/fuses, hasps), including durability/withstand criteria to prevent isolation failure and inadvertent re-energisation, in addition to the testing and earthing steps.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while electrical work is being carried out. The policy directly addresses this obligation by requiring isolation points to be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to operate, and withstand conditions that could cause isolation failure, with examples such as lockable switches and lockout devices. This is functionally aligned with the provision’s requirement and describes controls that would achieve it.

matches.target_title: 190 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) A designer of plant must ensure that the design provides for any operator's controls for the plant to be: 

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and   
(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for operator's controls that: 

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: (i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation, require deliberate action to operate, and be robust against conditions that could cause isolation failure (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The PROVISION requires operator controls to be located/guarded to prevent unintentional activation and to be able to be locked into the "off" position to enable disconnection of all motive power. These requirements are functionally aligned: both address preventing unintended activation/energisation and enabling lockout in the off state. While the POLICY includes additional detail about deliberate action and robustness, the PROVISION’s requirements for guarding against unintentional activation and lockability into off position satisfy the core intent of preventing inadvertent re-energisation via secure isolation/lockout-capable controls.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment is isolated/disconnected and, once isolated, is not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or is replaced/removed. The policy addresses isolation points having control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices), which supports maintaining isolation, but it does not explicitly require (1) that unsafe equipment must be isolated, nor (2) that it must not be reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/removed. Therefore the policy only partially addresses the regulatory obligation and is not fully compliant.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that when electrical equipment is identified as unsafe it must be immediately disconnected/isolated, and must not be reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and confirmed safe, or else replaced or permanently removed from service. Include clear responsibilities, tagging/lockout steps, and verification/authorization requirements before re-energisation.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 222 Industrial robots
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies to a person with management or control of an industrial robot or other remotely or automatically energised plant at a workplace.

(2) The person must not direct or allow a worker to work in the immediate vicinity of the plant if it could start without warning and cause a hazard, unless suitable control measures are in place to control the risks to health and safety. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(3) If the remote or automatic energising of the plant could lead to risks to health and safety, the person must ensure that access to the area in the immediate vicinity of the plant is controlled at all times: (a) by isolating the area; or (b) by: (i) providing interlocked guards; or (ii) if a risk remains, providing presence-sensing devices; or (iii) if a risk then remains, providing permit to work systems.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both address preventing hazardous unexpected energisation/start-up of plant and controlling the risk to workers in the immediate vicinity. However, the POLICY focuses on electrical isolation points having control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices requiring deliberate action and robust against failure). The PROVISION requires suitable control measures where plant could start without warning, and specifically requires controlled access to the area via isolating the area and/or interlocked guarding, presence-sensing devices, and permit-to-work systems. The POLICY does not address controlling access to the immediate vicinity, interlocked guards, presence-sensing devices, or permit-to-work systems, and it is limited to electrical isolation mechanisms rather than broader controls for remotely/automatically energised plant. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully meet the PROVISION’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Expand the POLICY to require, where plant can start without warning, that access to the immediate vicinity is controlled at all times by: (1) isolating the area and/or (2) providing interlocked guards; if residual risk remains, add presence-sensing devices; if risk still remains, implement permit-to-work systems. Also explicitly cover remotely/automatically energised plant (e.g., industrial robots) and prohibit directing/allowing work nearby unless these controls are in place.

source_text:
To meet the legislative requirements the following must be considered and addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment prior to carrying out the work:

a. The area where the electrical work is carried out is clear of obstructions so far as to allow for ease of access and exit   
b. The point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its supply is: Clearly marked and labelled, and Cleared of obstructions so as to allow easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and – Capable of being operated quickly c. The person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that no person/plant/thing comes within an unsafe distance of overhead or underground electric lines, and if that is not reasonably practicable, to conduct a risk assessment for the proposed work and implement control measures consistent with the risk assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. The policy text focuses on activity risk assessment items for electrical work such as keeping the work area and isolation/disconnection point clear, ensuring the isolation point is labelled and operable quickly, and requiring authorization after consultation. It does not address maintaining safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, does not specify triggers/criteria for when safe distance is not reasonably practicable, and does not require controls consistent with the risk assessment and electricity supply authority requirements. Therefore it does not adequately fulfill the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Activity Risk Assessment and associated procedure to explicitly: (1) identify overhead and underground electric lines and define/require maintaining prescribed safe approach distances; (2) require a specific risk assessment when safe distances cannot be maintained; (3) mandate implementation of control measures aligned to the risk assessment (e.g., de-energisation, isolation, barriers/exclusion zones, spotters, permits, signage, equipment limits); and (4) require consultation/coordination with the electricity supply authority and compliance with its requirements (including any permits/approvals) where it is responsible for the line.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated from supply and, once isolated, is not reconnected until repaired/tested safe or is replaced/permanently removed. The policy focuses on pre-work Activity Risk Assessment items (clear access/egress, isolation point labeling/accessibility/quick operation, and authorization/consultation) but does not require that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated, nor does it prohibit reconnection until repair/testing, nor require replacement/removal. Therefore it does not satisfy the core control obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment be immediately disconnected/isolated from its electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or alternatively replaced or permanently removed from use. Define criteria/process for determining 'unsafe' (reasonable grounds), assign responsibility (PCBU/competent person), and include verification/recordkeeping steps.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The policy text focuses on pre-work Activity Risk Assessment controls (clear access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation point, and authorisation after consultation). It does not state the core legal threshold that energised work must not be carried out unless one of the provision’s necessity exceptions applies, nor does it require documenting/justifying that exception. Therefore, while related (both govern conditions around electrical work and isolation/authorisation), the policy does not fully implement the provision’s prohibition/exception test for energised work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/ARA requirements to explicitly: (1) prohibit electrical work on energised equipment by default; (2) require confirmation and documentation that at least one of the provision’s conditions (a)-(d) applies before any energised work proceeds; (3) require consideration and documentation that de-energised work is not reasonably practicable (where relevant); and (4) specify that any energised testing/work permitted under the exceptions is limited to what is necessary and is captured in the authorisation/consultation process.

matches.target_title: 40 Duty in relation to general workplace facilities

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets requirements for an Activity Risk Assessment and workplace conditions (clear access/egress, unobstructed isolation points) that align with the PROVISION’s duties to ensure safe entry/exit, movement, and adequate space/layout without risk to health and safety. However, the POLICY only addresses obstruction-free access/egress in the electrical work area and access to isolation/disconnection points, plus an authorization/consultation step. It does not address several mandatory workplace-condition elements in the PROVISION, including: (c) safe floors/surfaces; (d) lighting for safe work/movement/evacuation; (e) ventilation; (f) managing extremes of heat/cold; and (g) managing risks when working near essential services (beyond electrical isolation access). Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s broader workplace layout and environmental condition requirements.
compliance.remediation: Expand the Activity Risk Assessment and associated controls to explicitly cover: (1) workspace adequacy (space to work safely) and safe movement/egress under normal and emergency conditions; (2) floor/surface condition checks (slip/trip, stability, maintenance); (3) lighting adequacy for task performance, movement, and emergency evacuation; (4) ventilation adequacy (including fumes/dust/heat load where relevant); (5) controls for extremes of heat/cold (PPE, breaks, hydration, warming/cooling, monitoring); and (6) risks from working near essential services (identify services, exclusion zones, permits, isolation/lockout where applicable). Document these checks and controls as required steps prior to commencing work.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY sets pre-work Activity Risk Assessment requirements focused on access/egress, isolation/disconnection point labeling and accessibility, and authorization/consultation. The PROVISION requires that during energised electrical work, the PCBU ensures all persons are prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The POLICY does not address controls for preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, supervision, signage, access control, PPE, or procedures specific to energised work). Therefore it does not fulfill the PROVISION’s obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to include specific requirements for energised electrical work that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components, such as: establishing and enforcing exclusion zones and access control; installing insulating barriers/covers/screens; applying lockable physical guards where practicable; using warning signage and spotters where needed; specifying required PPE and insulated tools; ensuring only authorised/competent persons are present; and documenting these controls in the Activity Risk Assessment and work method statement for energised work.

source_title: 7.2 Application to undertake energised electrical work
source_text:
To carry out energised electrical work, electrical workers must complete an Application to undertake energised electrical work (CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure Appendix 4).

A completed application must be endorsed by the electrical workers line manager and then submitted to the applicable Group Leader and BU Director for consideration and approval.

The purpose of the application form is to provide senior management with visibility of what if any energised electrical work is being conducted within a business unit and to ensure that the work:

 will be conducted by a competent person,   
 is justified and meets the energised electrical work exemptions under s157 of the WHS Regulations, and   
 will be conducted in a safe manner.

What to consider when completing an application?

When completing the application consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment/s need to be attached to the application.   
d. Does the risk assessment address all the considerations documented in Part 3 of the application form?   
e. Do you have a current and approved Verification of electrical competency form? Does it cover the electrical work listed in the application? Do you require an appropriate electrical licence to conduct the work – copy of the approved verification form needs to be attached to the application.   
f. Are there any unexpected events such as equipment failures, that may require you to perform non-routine energised electrical work? – make sure your application covers all routine and potential non-routine work to avoid having to apply for approval at short notice.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes an internal approval/application process for undertaking energised electrical work and requires attachments such as risk assessments and competency verification. This is functionally related to the PROVISION, which imposes mandatory preconditions before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; area and isolation point access/egress requirements; and authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace). The POLICY partially aligns with the PROVISION by requiring risk assessments/SWIs to be completed and attached, and by requiring management endorsement/approval (line manager, Group Leader, BU Director), which supports an authorisation concept. However, it does not explicitly require that: (1) the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (2) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly; (3) authorisation occurs after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace (the policy specifies internal management approvals but not the specific consultation requirement); and (4) the risk assessment is conducted by a competent person and recorded (it requires risk assessments but does not explicitly state competent-person conduct and recording, though attachments imply recording). Because several explicit pre-commencement controls in the PROVISION are not addressed, the POLICY is not fully compliant as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY/application requirements to explicitly mandate, before energised work starts: (a) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (b) confirmation the work area is clear of obstructions for safe access/egress; (c) confirmation the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, readily accessible for exit, and operable quickly (including noting the exception where applicable); and (d) documented authorisation after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace (e.g., workplace controller/facility manager), with evidence captured in the application/permit.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes an internal approval and documentation process for undertaking energised electrical work (application, endorsements, attachments such as risk assessments/SWIs and competency verification). This is functionally related to the PROVISION, which imposes mandatory conditions on how energised electrical work must be carried out (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, work in accordance with a compliant safe work method statement, and use of a safety observer unless an exception applies). However, the POLICY does not explicitly require: (1) that tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order; (2) that the work is carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement meeting the specific content requirements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review); (3) that a safety observer is present for energised work, including the observer’s competence and 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment, nor does it clearly document the limited exception for testing with a risk assessment showing no serious risk. The POLICY’s references to Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs partially align with the SWMS concept but do not ensure all SWMS elements required by the PROVISION are met.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY/application requirements to explicitly mandate: (a) confirmation and records that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable for the task, have been properly tested, and are maintained in good working order, and that workers use them properly; (b) a Safe Work Method Statement (or equivalent document) that includes all elements in subregulation (3) and is required for all energised electrical work; (c) a safety observer requirement for energised work, including observer competency and documented assessment within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation, plus clear criteria and documentation for the testing-only exception (risk assessment demonstrating no serious risk). Ensure the application form captures these items and requires evidence/attachments where appropriate.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes an internal approval and documentation process for undertaking energised electrical work and references that the work must be justified and meet the WHS Regulations s157 exemptions. The PROVISION (WHS Regs r157) imposes a substantive legal requirement that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the necessity/no-alternative conditions in (1)(a)-(d) applies. While the POLICY indicates that energised work must be justified and meet the exemptions, it does not explicitly require the application/approval process to confirm and record which specific r157(1) condition applies (a, b, c, or d), nor does it clearly prohibit energised work unless one of those conditions is met. Therefore, the POLICY is only partially aligned and does not fully operationalize the PROVISION’s threshold test as a mandatory gate.
compliance.remediation: Amend the application/approval requirements to explicitly: (1) prohibit energised electrical work unless at least one WHS Reg r157(1)(a)-(d) criterion is satisfied; (2) require the applicant to nominate the applicable criterion (a/b/c/d) and provide documented justification/evidence; (3) require approvers (line manager, Group Leader, BU Director) to verify and sign off that the nominated r157 criterion is met before approval; and (4) ensure testing under r155 is explicitly captured where r157(1)(c) is relied upon.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs the internal approval process for undertaking energised electrical work (application, endorsements, competency verification, and risk assessment attachments). The PROVISION imposes operational safety duties on the PCBU that must occur before and during electrical work: (1) ensure equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised before electrical work is carried out; and (2) ensure exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation. The POLICY references WHS Regulation 157 exemptions and discusses planning for de-energised vs energised work, competency, and risk assessments, but it does not explicitly require pre-work testing to determine energisation status, does not mandate treating exposed parts as energised until isolation and confirmation, and does not require earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Therefore, it does not fully address the specific controls required by the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/application requirements to explicitly mandate: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine whether it is energised (and reference the required testing method/process, e.g., per Reg 161); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage work, earthing of exposed parts after de-energisation. Ensure these items are captured as mandatory checklist fields in the application form and/or SWIs/risk assessments, with evidence/records required.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes an internal approval and competency/risk-assessment process for undertaking energised electrical work (application, endorsements, evidence of competency, and risk assessments). The PROVISION imposes an operational safety duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. While the POLICY supports safer energised work through governance and planning, it does not explicitly require or describe the specific control outcome mandated by the PROVISION (preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised components), nor does it mandate physical/administrative controls (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, supervision) to achieve that prevention during the work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/application requirements to explicitly require controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work, such as: documented isolation/exclusion zones; barriers/guards/covers or insulating mats/blankets; lockable enclosures where practicable; signage and access control to restrict non-essential persons; use of insulated tools/PPE appropriate to the task; a safety observer/spotter where required; and verification in the risk assessment/SWI that these controls are implemented and maintained for the duration of the work.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY governs when and how electrical workers may undertake energised electrical work, which is a subset of “electrical work” as defined in the PROVISION. A compliance check is necessary to ensure the POLICY aligns with the regulatory scope and exclusions for what constitutes “electrical work.” The POLICY does not define “electrical work” or acknowledge the PROVISION’s explicit exclusions (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs where safely performed by non-experts, certain supervised conduit work when wiring is not energised, supervised assistance without contact with energised equipment, and eligibility work other than on energised equipment). Because the POLICY broadly triggers an application process for “energised electrical work” without clarifying boundaries between regulated electrical work and excluded activities, it risks over-applying the approval requirement to tasks that are not “electrical work” under the PROVISION or are explicitly excluded. This is a misalignment with the regulatory definition/scope and could create unnecessary administrative burden and confusion about when the procedure applies.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/procedure to (1) incorporate or reference the regulatory definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions; (2) clearly state that the energised electrical work application requirement applies only to tasks that are “electrical work” and involve energised electrical equipment (and are not excluded activities); (3) add decision guidance/examples mapping common tasks to whether they are excluded vs. in-scope; and (4) ensure internal forms and training reflect these scope limits so approvals are not required for excluded activities.

matches.target_title: 87 Application for high risk work licence
154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a primary duty on the PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out while equipment is energised (i.e., energised work is prohibited unless an applicable exception under the Division applies). The policy establishes an approval and visibility process for undertaking energised electrical work and references WHS Regulations s157 exemptions, but it does not explicitly state the overarching requirement that energised electrical work must not be performed and is only permitted where the legal exemption/exception criteria are met and de-energisation is not reasonably practicable (or other applicable exception). By framing energised work as something that can be permitted via internal approval without clearly anchoring it to the legal prohibition and exception threshold, the policy does not fully ensure compliance with the provision’s requirement to prevent energised work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly state that electrical work must be performed de-energised and that energised electrical work is prohibited except where the WHS legal exception criteria are satisfied. Require documented justification that de-energisation is not reasonably practicable (or other applicable exception), and make approval contingent on verifying those criteria, including controls to ensure energised work does not occur outside approved exceptions.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per WHS risk management requirements). The policy establishes a governance and risk-control process specifically for energised electrical work, requiring prior application, management endorsement and senior approval, and requiring evidence of competency/licensing, justification under WHS Regulation exemptions, and documented risk assessments/SWIs. These measures are consistent with managing electrical risks and therefore functionally relate to the provision. While the provision is broader than energised work alone, the policy content provided aligns with and supports compliance for the energised electrical work scenario it covers.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

source_text:
To ensure that energised electrical work is carried out safely and risk minimised to yourself and others, the work must be performed:

a. By a competent person who has the tools, testing equipment and PPE that are suitable for the work, have been properly tested and are maintained in good working order;   
b. In accordance with a Safe Work Instruction prepared for the work; and   
c. Subject to the exception explained below – with a safety observer present who is competent: To implement control measures in an emergency, and – To rescue and resuscitate the worker who is carrying out the work if necessary, and Has been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person.

A safety observer is not required if the work consists only of testing and the risk assessment shows there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to implement the same obligations as the PROVISION for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE; documented safe work document; safety observer with specified competence; testing-only exception based on risk assessment). However, it is not fully compliant because: (1) The PROVISION requires the work be carried out in accordance with a "safe work method statement" (SWMS) with specific required contents (identify work; specify hazards/risks; describe control measures; describe implementation/monitoring/review). The POLICY instead refers to a "Safe Work Instruction" and does not state the mandatory SWMS content requirements. (2) The PROVISION imposes an additional duty to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; the POLICY does not include this obligation. (3) The testing-only exception in the PROVISION requires that the PCBU has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a); the POLICY mentions a risk assessment but does not specify that it must be conducted by/for the PCBU under the referenced regulation (or an equivalent formal requirement), creating a potential gap in accountability and process.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) require a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) for energised electrical work (or explicitly define the Safe Work Instruction as meeting SWMS requirements) and include the minimum SWMS elements: identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls will be implemented/monitored/reviewed; (2) add an explicit requirement that, so far as reasonably practicable, the business ensures workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; (3) tighten the testing-only safety observer exception to require a documented risk assessment conducted by the PCBU (and reference the applicable regulation/process) demonstrating no serious risk.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs how energised electrical work must be performed (competent person with suitable tools/PPE, Safe Work Instruction, and generally a competent safety observer unless testing-only with no serious risk). The PROVISION imposes additional pre-commencement duties on the PCBU for energised electrical work: a competent-person risk assessment that is recorded, ensuring the work area and isolation/disconnection point are clear of obstructions and suitably marked/labelled and operable quickly, and formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY does not explicitly require (1) a risk assessment (nor that it be recorded), (2) clearing obstructions for access/egress, (3) marking/labelling and ensuring accessibility/quick operability of the isolation point, or (4) authorisation after consultation. Therefore, while the POLICY addresses some safety controls, it does not cover several mandatory pre-commencement requirements in the PROVISION and is not compliant as a complete implementation of the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add a linked procedure/checklist) to require before energised electrical work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly (including noting the stated exception for supply-side/main switch situations where not reasonably accessible); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The policy focuses on requirements for performing energised electrical work (competent person, SWI, safety observer with an exception for testing-only work). It does not explicitly require pre-work testing to determine energisation status, does not state that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolation and confirmation, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, it does not fully meet the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that, before electrical work on electrical equipment begins, a competent person tests the equipment to determine whether it is energised (with testing performed in accordance with applicable procedures/regs). Add requirements that (1) all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (2) any high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after being de-energised. Ensure these steps are incorporated into the Safe Work Instruction and training/competency expectations.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.
