matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy focuses on who may perform energised work (competent person with suitable tools/PPE), use of a Safe Work Instruction, and (generally) having a competent safety observer, with an exception for testing where a risk assessment shows no serious risk. However, it does not explicitly require controls to prevent all persons (including bystanders/other workers) from inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., barriers, exclusion zones, insulation/covering, access control, signage, guarding). The presence of a safety observer and PPE may reduce risk but does not, on its face, ensure prevention of inadvertent contact by all persons as required by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/SWI requirements to explicitly mandate controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components by any person during energised work, such as: establishing and enforcing exclusion zones; barricading/guarding and insulating or shrouding exposed live parts; access control/permit-to-work and supervision; warning signage; and verification that these controls are in place before and during the task (including when the safety observer exception applies).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy sets specific risk controls for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/maintained tools, testing equipment and PPE; work performed under a Safe Work Instruction; use of a competent safety observer with rescue/resuscitation capability unless testing-only with no serious risk per risk assessment). These measures constitute a structured approach to identifying and controlling electrical risks and align with the requirement to manage electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

matches.target_title: 299 Safe work method statement required for high risk construction work
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must, before high risk construction work commences, ensure that a safe work method statement for the proposed work: 

(a) is prepared; or (b) has already been prepared by another person. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the work that is high risk construction work; and   
(b) specify hazards relating to the high risk construction work and risks to health and safety associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(3) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) be prepared taking into account all relevant matters, including: 

(i) circumstances at the workplace that may affect the way in which the high risk construction work is carried out; and   
(ii) if the high risk construction work is carried out in connection with a construction project—the WHS management plan that has been prepared for the workplace; and 

(b) be set out and expressed in a way that is readily accessible and understandable to persons who use it.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it establishes CSIRO’s equivalent process (SWI) to meet the WHS requirement for a SWMS for high-risk work (including energised electrical work). However, the PROVISION applies specifically to “high risk construction work” and requires that, before such work commences, a SWMS is prepared (or already prepared), and that it includes: identification of the high-risk construction work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented, monitored and reviewed; plus preparation considering workplace circumstances and (if applicable) the WHS management plan, and that it is accessible/understandable. The POLICY/SWI requirements cover describing the work, hazards, safety requirements, authorisation, emergencies, and review/revision, but do not explicitly require (1) preparation before commencement, (2) explicit identification that the work is “high risk construction work”, (3) description of control measures and how they are implemented/monitored/reviewed, (4) consideration of workplace circumstances and WHS management plan (for construction projects), or (5) accessibility/understandability requirements. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s mandatory SWMS content and process requirements where the work is high risk construction work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SWI/SWMS procedure for any work that is “high risk construction work” to explicitly require: (1) a SWMS (or SWI that meets SWMS requirements) is in place before work commences; (2) the statement identifies the work as high risk construction work; (3) it specifies hazards/risks and describes the control measures; (4) it describes how controls will be implemented, monitored and reviewed; (5) it is prepared considering workplace circumstances and, for construction projects, the WHS management plan; and (6) it is set out in a readily accessible and understandable form for workers.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it is intended to implement the WHS requirement that energised electrical work be carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), using CSIRO’s Safe Work Instruction (SWI) as the equivalent. The POLICY is partially aligned with the SWMS content requirements in subregulation (3): it requires describing the work (3(a)), specifying hazards (part of 3(b)), and describing health and safety requirements (partly aligns with 3(c)). It also requires emergency steps, which is additional but not conflicting. However, the POLICY does not explicitly require: (1) that the SWI specify risks associated with the hazards (3(b)); (2) that it describe measures to control the risks (3(c)) in a clear, explicit way; and (3) that it describe how risk controls are implemented, monitored, and reviewed (3(d))—it only states SWIs must be reviewed/revised when controls change, which is not equivalent to documenting implementation/monitoring/review arrangements within the SWI. Additionally, the PROVISION imposes requirements not addressed in the POLICY: work must be carried out by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE (1)(a) and ensuring proper use (2), and generally requires a safety observer with specified competence and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment (1)(c), (4), with a limited exception (5). The POLICY focuses on SWI content and review but does not incorporate these operational requirements, so it does not fully fulfill the regulatory obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly include: (1) documenting hazards AND the risks associated with those hazards; (2) documenting specific risk control measures; (3) documenting how controls will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed (not just that the SWI is periodically reviewed). Also add explicit requirements that energised electrical work is performed only by competent persons using suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE, with processes to ensure proper use. Include the safety observer requirement (and the testing-only/risk-assessed exception) and specify observer competence and 12-month rescue/resuscitation competency assessment.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is intended to operationalise WHS requirements for energised electrical work via CSIRO Safe Work Instructions (SWIs), which are positioned as the equivalent of SWMS. The PROVISION, however, imposes specific pre-commencement duties on the PCBU for energised electrical work: (1)(a) conduct a risk assessment; (1)(b) ensure the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) ensure the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly; and (1)(d) authorise the work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace. It also requires the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded (2). The POLICY requires SWIs to describe hazards identified in a risk assessment and approval/authorisation arrangements, and to include safety considerations and emergency steps, plus review/revision requirements. However, it does not explicitly require: clearing obstructions in the work area; marking/labeling and keeping clear the isolation/disconnection point and ensuring it can be operated quickly; consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace prior to authorisation; nor that the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded. Because these specific preconditions are not clearly mandated in the POLICY, it does not fully cover the PROVISION’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements (and/or associated procedure/checklist) for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate, before work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (including addressing the exception scenario in subregulation (3) where relevant); and (4) formal authorisation occurs only after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records of the consultation/authorisation retained.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (general risk management). The policy establishes a structured risk-control mechanism for a subset of electrical risk (energised electrical work) by requiring a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) aligned to CSIRO risk management, including hazard identification, safety requirements/controls, authorisation arrangements, emergency steps, and periodic review when controls change. These elements are consistent with managing electrical risks via identifying hazards, implementing and maintaining controls, and reviewing controls, which aligns with the intent of Part 3.1 risk management duties.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes that energised electrical work (high-risk work) requires a Safe Work Instruction (SWI), which is presented as CSIRO’s equivalent to a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS). The PROVISION imposes recordkeeping and accessibility obligations for copies of a risk assessment and/or SWMS: retention for specified periods, extended retention after a notifiable incident, ready accessibility to workers, and availability for inspection. The POLICY does not address any of these requirements (how long to keep the SWI/SWMS or risk assessment, ensuring worker access during the retention period, or making it available for inspection). Therefore, while the POLICY is related in subject matter (SWMS/SWI for energised electrical work), it does not fulfill the PROVISION’s recordkeeping/accessibility obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or an associated procedure) to explicitly require: (1) keeping a copy of the energised electrical work risk assessment for at least 28 days after the work is completed; (2) keeping a copy of the SWMS/SWI until the work is completed; (3) if a notifiable incident occurs, retaining the relevant risk assessment and SWMS/SWI for at least 2 years after the incident; (4) ensuring copies are readily accessible to workers engaged in the electrical work for the required retention period; and (5) ensuring copies are available for inspection under the Act for the required retention period.

matches.target_title: 300 Compliance with safe work method statement
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must put in place arrangements for ensuring that high risk construction work is carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If high risk construction work is not carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work, the person must ensure that the work: 

(a) is stopped immediately or as soon as it is safe to do so; and (b) resumed only in accordance with the statement. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it establishes CSIRO’s internal equivalent (SWI) to the WHS requirement to conduct certain high-risk work in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and it mandates SWIs for high-risk work including energised electrical work. However, the PROVISION imposes two specific duties on the PCBU for high risk construction work: (1) put arrangements in place to ensure the work is carried out in accordance with the SWMS, and (2) if work is not carried out in accordance with the SWMS, ensure it is stopped immediately/as soon as safe and only resumed in accordance with the SWMS. The POLICY covers preparation/content and review of SWIs but does not explicitly require (a) arrangements to ensure work is carried out in accordance with the SWI/SWMS, nor (b) an explicit stop-work and resume-only-when-compliant requirement when deviations occur. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s operational enforcement requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/SWI framework to explicitly require: (1) documented arrangements to ensure high risk construction work is performed in accordance with the applicable SWMS (or SWI as the equivalent), including supervision/verification and worker briefing/acknowledgement; and (2) a mandatory stop-work process when work is not being performed in accordance with the SWMS/SWI, with clear authority to stop work and a requirement that work only resumes once brought back into compliance with the SWMS/SWI.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, necessity to perform the work properly, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy focuses on requiring a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for energised electrical work and specifies SWI content and review requirements, but it does not state or enforce the threshold prohibition/permission criteria for when energised work may be performed (i.e., it does not require de-energisation by default or require documenting/justifying that one of the subregulation (1) conditions is met before proceeding). Therefore, while related, the policy does not fully implement the provision’s core obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/SWI requirements to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless one of the WHS Reg conditions is satisfied, and require the SWI (or an attached risk assessment/authorisation) to document which condition applies (1)(a)-(d), including justification that there is no reasonable alternative where relevant. Include an approval/authorisation gate that verifies the applicable condition before work starts, and ensure testing is aligned to reg 155 where applicable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY establishes a requirement to use a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for energised electrical work and specifies SWI content (authorisations, task description, hazards, safety requirements, emergency steps, and review). The PROVISION imposes specific operational duties before and during electrical work: (1) testing electrical equipment by a competent person to determine whether it is energised before work starts; (2) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. The POLICY does not explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised' rule, and does not require earthing of de-energised high-voltage exposed parts. While these could be implicitly covered under 'hazards' and 'health and safety considerations,' the absence of explicit requirements means the POLICY does not clearly ensure the PROVISION’s obligations are met.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements (and/or a mandatory SWI template for energised electrical work) to explicitly include: (1) a step requiring testing of the equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine energisation status (with reference to applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage work, a step requiring exposed parts to be earthed after de-energising. Ensure SWI review triggers include changes to testing/isolation/earthing controls.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the POLICY governs controls for “energised electrical work” and “electrical work,” while the PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. A compliance check is necessary to ensure the POLICY’s scope and requirements align with the regulatory definition. The POLICY does not define “electrical work” or acknowledge the exclusions listed in the PROVISION (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, certain supervised assistance, etc.). As written, the POLICY could be interpreted to require SWIs for activities that are not “electrical work” under the PROVISION, creating over-application and potential inconsistency in classification and governance. Additionally, the POLICY focuses on SWI requirements for energised electrical work but does not explicitly tie its scope to the regulatory definition of electrical work, which is necessary for clear applicability.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY/SWI framework to explicitly adopt or reference the regulatory definition of “electrical work” and list (or reference) the key exclusions, clarifying when an SWI is required versus when tasks fall outside “electrical work.” Add a scope/applicability section that distinguishes: (1) energised electrical work (always SWI), (2) other electrical work (SWI if high-risk per CSIRO risk procedure), and (3) excluded activities (no SWI unless a separate risk assessment deems it necessary).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to implement the PROVISION’s requirements for energised electrical work, including when a safety observer is required and the observer’s competency/CPR recency. The POLICY aligns with the exemption for testing based on risk assessment and includes the 12‑month rescue/resuscitation assessment requirement. However, it does not include several mandatory elements in the PROVISION: (1) ensuring the work is carried out by a competent person with suitable, properly tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) ensuring the work is carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS); (3) specifying the required SWMS contents (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); and (4) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. Because these obligations are absent from the POLICY text, the POLICY is not fully compliant with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly require: (1) energised electrical work be performed by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) the work be conducted in accordance with a SWMS prepared for the work; (3) the SWMS include the required elements (work identification, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed); and (4) the PCBU ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are related because both address controls required before/during energised electrical work. However, the POLICY focuses on requiring a competent safety observer (with specific competency/CPR requirements and observation limits) and only mentions a risk assessment as a basis for exempting the observer for testing. The PROVISION imposes additional mandatory pre-commencement duties on the PCBU: (1) conduct a risk assessment (by a competent person and recorded), (2) ensure the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit, (3) ensure the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly (with a limited exception), and (4) authorise the work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace. These obligations are not addressed in the POLICY text (recording of risk assessment, access/egress clearance, isolation point marking/access/quick operation, and authorisation/consultation), so the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a pre-work risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of quick operation (and document the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records of the authorisation/consultation.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used.

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is:
