matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the necessity conditions in (1)(a)-(d) is met (including testing under (c)). The policy focuses on requiring a safety observer for energised work (with a limited exception for testing where a risk assessment shows an observer is not required), but it does not state or require that energised work may only proceed when one of the regulatory necessity criteria (health and safety necessity, necessary to perform properly, testing required under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative) is satisfied. Because the policy permits energised work subject to observer/risk-assessment controls without explicitly restricting it to the regulation’s allowed circumstances, it does not fully implement the provision’s threshold prohibition/permission structure.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless at least one of the conditions in (1)(a)-(d) is met, and require documentation/approval demonstrating which condition applies (including explicitly linking any testing exception to testing required under reg 155 where applicable). Retain the safety observer and risk assessment controls as additional requirements once the threshold condition is satisfied.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting an exposed energised component. The policy focuses on requiring a competent safety observer (with rescue/CPR competence) during energised work, with an exception for testing where risk is managed. While a safety observer may help respond to incidents, the policy does not explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent contact (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, guarding, access control, supervision of non-workers). Therefore, the policy does not fully address the prevention obligation in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require measures that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work, such as establishing and enforcing exclusion zones, physical barriers/guards, insulating covers/mats, lockable access control, signage, restricting access to authorised persons only, and procedures to ensure bystanders/other workers cannot approach or touch exposed parts. Include a requirement to verify these controls are in place before and throughout energised work.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

source_text:
Section 6.3 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides additional guidance on requirements and recommendations to comply with the legislation regarding carrying out energised electrical work, as well as:

 Hazards indirectly caused by electricity, such as conductive materials;  Work positioning that minimises the risk of inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts; and  Safety barriers and signs that must be erected when work is carried out.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a broad duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1. The policy text references Section 6.3 of a Code of Practice and lists specific guidance items (indirect electrical hazards, work positioning, safety barriers/signs) focused on energised electrical work. While this guidance is relevant to managing electrical risks, the policy does not state that the PCBU must manage electrical risks generally, does not reference compliance with Part 3.1 risk management requirements, and appears limited to a subset of electrical risk controls. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the provision’s broader obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require the PCBU to manage all electrical risks to health and safety at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1 (e.g., identify hazards, assess risks where required, implement and maintain controls using the hierarchy of control, and review controls). Expand scope beyond energised electrical work to include design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations, and link the Code of Practice guidance as supporting measures within the Part 3.1 risk management process.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text provides guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work, including hazards indirectly caused by electricity (e.g., conductive materials), work positioning to minimise inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts, and the use of safety barriers and signs. The PROVISION text sets mandatory pre-commencement requirements for energised electrical work: conducting and recording a competent-person risk assessment, ensuring the work area and isolation/disconnection point are clear of obstructions for access/egress, ensuring the isolation point is clearly marked and quickly operable, and requiring authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The POLICY does not address several core mandatory elements in the PROVISION (risk assessment conducted/recorded by a competent person; clearing obstructions for access/exit; marking/labeling and operability of isolation point; formal authorisation/consultation). Therefore, the POLICY as written does not demonstrate coverage of the PROVISION’s required controls and cannot be considered compliant with it.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a pre-work risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area to be cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point to be clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of quick operation (and document the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Ensure these requirements are implemented via checklists/permits and training.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy text (Section 6.3 guidance) addresses measures directly aligned with this duty, including work positioning to minimise inadvertent contact and the use of safety barriers and signs when work is carried out. These controls are functionally aimed at preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts, satisfying the core requirement of the provision.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is guidance on managing electrical risks when carrying out energised electrical work, including hazards indirectly caused by electricity, safe work positioning, and use of safety barriers/signs. The PROVISION imposes specific mandatory duties before electrical work: (1) ensure equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised; (2) treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. While the POLICY addresses related risk controls, it does not explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person, the 'treat as energised until proven otherwise' rule, or earthing of de-energised high-voltage exposed parts. Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before electrical work to confirm energised/de-energised status (per applicable testing requirements); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. Include clear responsibilities, step-by-step verification/isolation/earthing processes, and references to the relevant testing method requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is guidance about managing electrical risks when carrying out energised electrical work (e.g., indirect electrical hazards, safe work positioning, and use of safety barriers/signage). The PROVISION imposes a primary legal obligation: energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health and safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). The POLICY does not state or evidence the required threshold decision (i.e., that energised work is prohibited unless one of the exceptions applies), nor does it require documenting/justifying that an exception applies before proceeding. Therefore, the POLICY does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s core prohibition-and-exceptions requirement.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work except where one of the PROVISION’s conditions (a)-(d) is met. Require a pre-work assessment and documented justification identifying which condition applies, including confirmation that de-energised work is not reasonably practicable where relevant. Include controls/procedures for authorisation of energised work and ensure testing is addressed consistently with the allowance in subregulation (2).

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that persons/plant/things do not come within an unsafe distance of overhead or underground electric lines, and if that is not reasonably practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement control measures consistent with the assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. The policy text discusses guidance for managing electrical risks during energised electrical work, including indirect hazards (conductive materials), work positioning to avoid inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts, and erection of safety barriers and signs. While these topics are related to electrical risk controls, the policy does not explicitly address maintaining safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, does not state the 'so far as reasonably practicable' requirement, and does not include the mandated fallback process (risk assessment and controls aligned to the assessment and supply authority requirements) when safe distance cannot be ensured. Therefore, the policy is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the specific obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) maintaining prescribed/defined safe (unsafe) approach distances from overhead and underground electric lines for all persons, plant and things, so far as reasonably practicable; (2) where safe distance is not reasonably practicable, conducting a documented risk assessment for the proposed work; (3) implementing and documenting control measures consistent with the risk assessment; and (4) consulting and complying with any applicable electricity supply authority requirements for the relevant line (including permits, isolation, de-energisation, spotters, exclusion zones, and signage/barriers as applicable).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text is guidance intended to support compliance when carrying out energised electrical work, addressing hazards indirectly caused by electricity (e.g., conductive materials), work positioning to minimise inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts, and the need for safety barriers and signs. The PROVISION imposes specific mandatory requirements on energised electrical work: it must be performed by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) meeting detailed content requirements; and (subject to exceptions) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. The POLICY does not explicitly require or evidence these mandated elements (competent person criteria, tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS preparation and required contents, safety observer presence/competency/assessment, or the risk assessment basis for any observer exemption). Therefore, while related, the POLICY as written is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) work only by a competent person; (2) use of tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (3) a SWMS prepared for the work that identifies the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (4) a safety observer present unless the work is testing-only and a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk; (5) safety observer competency and documented assessment within the last 12 months for rescue and resuscitation; and (6) a requirement (so far as reasonably practicable) that workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a primary duty: the PCBU must ensure electrical work is not carried out on energised electrical equipment (subject to any exceptions in the Division). The policy text references guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work and lists control measures (hazards, positioning, barriers/signs), but it does not state the required prohibition/requirement to avoid energised work or the obligation to ensure work is not performed while energised. Because the policy focuses on how to manage energised work rather than establishing the default rule that energised work must not occur, it does not fully address the provision’s core requirement.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that electrical work must not be performed on energised electrical equipment, except where permitted under the applicable Division (and specify the conditions/approval process for any exception). Retain the listed controls as additional requirements for any permitted energised work, and add clear procedures for de-energisation, isolation/lockout-tagout, verification of de-energised state, and documentation/authorization.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY text provides guidance on managing electrical risks during energised electrical work (e.g., hazards from conductive materials, safe work positioning, and use of barriers/signage). The PROVISION imposes a specific duty: when equipment is de-energised for electrical work, it must be prevented from being inadvertently re-energised (i.e., isolation/lockout-tagout type controls). The POLICY does not address preventing inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment, so it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION’s requirement.
compliance.remediation: Add/implement controls and procedures to ensure de-energised electrical equipment cannot be inadvertently re-energised during work, such as documented isolation procedures, lockout/tagout, securing isolation points, verification of de-energisation, control of keys/access, signage specific to isolation, and supervision/auditing to ensure the controls are followed.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

source_title: 7.7 Energised electrical work – testing and fault-finding

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised. It also requires exposed parts to be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and requires each high-voltage exposed part to be earthed after being de-energised. The policy is broadly about testing and fault-finding and includes requirements for competent/trained workers and safe testing practices, but it does not explicitly mandate a pre-work test to determine whether equipment is energised before any electrical work begins. It also does not clearly state the legal rule that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, nor does it include the specific requirement to earth each high-voltage exposed part after de-energisation. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment begins, a competent person must test the equipment to determine whether it is energised (and document/verify this step as part of the work process). Add an explicit rule that all exposed parts are to be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised. Add a specific requirement that each high-voltage exposed part must be earthed after being de-energised, including procedural guidance (e.g., approved earthing devices, sequence, verification) consistent with applicable regulations/standards.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets internal requirements for carrying out testing and fault-finding on/near energised electrical equipment, including tools/test equipment/PPE suitability and maintenance, competency, and use of safety observers—topics directly regulated by the PROVISION.

However, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s mandatory elements:
1) Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS): The PROVISION requires energised electrical work to be carried out in accordance with a SWMS prepared for the work, and specifies minimum SWMS content (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed). The POLICY discusses risk assessment and procedures but does not explicitly require a SWMS for energised electrical work nor include the required SWMS content requirements.
2) Safety observer requirement: The PROVISION makes a safety observer mandatory for energised electrical work, except where the work is only testing and a specific risk assessment shows no serious risk. The POLICY states “Use a safety observer, if required by the risk assessment,” which is weaker than the default mandatory requirement and does not clearly reflect the limited exception.
3) Safety observer competence/rescue & resuscitation currency: The PROVISION requires the safety observer to be competent and assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate. The POLICY mentions a safety observer may be required but does not specify these competence/assessment requirements.
4) Ensuring proper use of tools/PPE: The POLICY requires ensuring workers have suitable, tested, maintained tools/PPE and provides training/instruction on PPE use, but it does not clearly impose the PCBU duty to ensure (so far as reasonably practicable) that tools/testing equipment/PPE are used properly (beyond PPE training).
compliance.remediation:
Amend the POLICY to explicitly require that all energised electrical work is performed in accordance with a SWMS prepared for the work, and specify SWMS minimum contents (work identification; hazards/risks; control measures; implementation/monitoring/review).

Revise the safety observer section to align with the regulation: require a safety observer for energised electrical work by default, and only allow the exception where (i) the work consists only of testing and (ii) a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk.

Add explicit safety observer qualification requirements: the observer must be competent and have been assessed within the last 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate.

Add an explicit duty/process for the PCBU/supervisor to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use tools, testing equipment and PPE (e.g., supervision, pre-start checks, audits/spot checks, enforcement).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The PROVISION requires the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment (plug-in equipment used in harsh/damaging environments) is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and to keep specified testing records (name, date, outcome, next test due) until the next test or disposal. The POLICY focuses on safe methods and controls for electrical testing and fault-finding (especially energised work), including instrument standards, PPE, barriers, authorised access, and worker competence. However, it does not establish a program for regular inspection/testing of plug-in equipment in specified environments, does not state inspection/testing frequency/trigger criteria, and does not require keeping the mandated testing records or tagging with the required fields. Therefore it does not fulfill the core obligations of the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Add a section covering in-scope equipment (plug-in equipment used in environments likely to cause damage), require regular inspection and testing by a competent person (including defined intervals or a risk-based schedule), require pre-use inspection of new/unused equipment for obvious damage, and implement recordkeeping that captures: tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via compliant tags and/or a register) retained until next test or disposal. Also reference the prohibition on using unsafe equipment and link to the organization’s process for removing/isolating failed items.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets procedural controls for testing/fault-finding in an energised environment (i.e., energised electrical work) and therefore should address the preconditions required before energised work commences. However, the POLICY does not fully meet the PROVISION’s specific mandatory pre-start requirements for energised electrical work: (1)(a) it references a risk assessment only indirectly (e.g., safety observer “if required by the risk assessment”) and does not explicitly require that a risk assessment be conducted before energised work commences; (2) it does not require the risk assessment to be conducted by a competent person and recorded as required by (2); (3) it does not require the work area to be clear of obstructions to allow easy access/exit as required by (1)(b); (4) it does not require the isolation/disconnection point to be clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly as required by (1)(c); and (5) it does not require formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace as required by (1)(d). While the POLICY includes related controls (barriers, instrument checks, authorised persons in the area, coordination procedures), these do not substitute for the explicit preconditions in the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to explicitly require, before any energised electrical work (including energised testing/fault-finding) commences: (1) a risk assessment is conducted, by a competent person, and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to ensure easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is identified and verified as clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (and include the exception scenario consistent with subregulation (3) where applicable); and (4) the energised work is formally authorised after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Add procedural steps/checklists and recordkeeping requirements to evidence compliance.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits energised electrical work unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, necessary to perform properly, necessary for testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy focuses on how to conduct testing and fault-finding safely (controls, barriers, authorised access, suitable instruments/PPE, competency, restoration, coordination), and it states that de-energised methods should be considered before energised methods are adopted. However, it does not explicitly require that energised work must not be carried out unless one of the provision’s necessity criteria is met, nor does it require documenting/justifying that criterion (e.g., that there is no reasonable alternative). Therefore, while it supports safe execution, it does not fully implement the legal threshold/permissioning condition for when energised work is allowed.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly prohibit energised electrical work unless at least one of the permitted conditions is satisfied (health/safety necessity, necessary to perform properly, necessary testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Require a pre-work decision/authorisation step that documents: (1) why de-energised methods are not reasonably practicable, (2) which specific condition (a)-(d) is being relied upon, and (3) approval by an authorised person before energised testing/fault-finding proceeds.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. The policy directly addresses this by requiring identification of exposed conductive parts that could become energised, installation of temporary or fixed barriers to prevent inadvertent contact, restricting access to the immediate work area to authorised persons only, and requiring appropriate insulated tools/PPE and ongoing hazard review. These controls are functionally aligned with preventing inadvertent contact and associated electrical risk during energised work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a general duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks (including testing) in accordance with WHS risk management requirements. The policy directly addresses managing electrical risks during testing and fault-finding by requiring de-energised methods where possible, specifying controls for energised testing (barriers, authorised access, insulated/rated tools and probes, PPE, safety observer based on risk assessment, ongoing hazard review), ensuring equipment suitability/maintenance/calibration, requiring worker competence/training, and restoring equipment to a safe condition. These measures constitute practical risk controls consistent with managing electrical risks under Part 3.1.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific obligation on the person with management or control of a workplace to (1) ensure residual current devices (RCDs) are tested regularly by a competent person to confirm effective operation, and (2) keep records of all RCD testing (except daily testing) until the next test or permanent removal. The policy text addresses general electrical testing and fault-finding practices, competence, instrument standards, PPE, and safety controls, but it does not specifically require regular RCD testing, does not define an RCD testing regime, and does not require retention of RCD test records as specified. Therefore, the policy does not demonstrate compliance with the provision’s RCD testing and recordkeeping requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) regular testing of all workplace RCDs by a competent person (define frequency consistent with applicable regulatory/industry requirements), and (2) retention of records for all non-daily RCD tests until the RCD is next tested or permanently removed from use. Implement a register/log for RCD test results (device ID/location, date, tester competency, method/results, corrective actions) and assign responsibility to the workplace controller for scheduling and record retention.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

source_text:
When purchasing electrical equipment, you must ensure that the equipment meets applicable Australian Standards prior to it being commissioned such as AS/NZS 3820 Essential safety requirements for electrical equipment or equivalent international standards recognised within Australia. Electrical equipment that comply with Australia Standards will have a regulatory compliance mark (a triangle with a circled tick inside) displayed on the equipment.

This is a particular issue for equipment that is purchased internationally, so inspect newly acquired electrical equipment and if there is any doubt engage an electrician to inspect the equipment.

If the equipment is electrically unsafe return it to the supplier or arrange for the equipment to be modified and certified to meet Australian Standards.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses ensuring purchased electrical equipment meets Australian Standards before commissioning, inspecting newly acquired equipment (especially imported), engaging an electrician if in doubt, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The PROVISION requires (1) regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in harsh/damaging environments; (2) for new/unused equipment, inspection for obvious damage before use; and (3)-(4) keeping specified testing records until next test/removal. The POLICY partially aligns with subregulation (2)(b) by requiring inspection of newly acquired equipment and not using/rectifying unsafe equipment, but it does not establish a program for regular inspection/testing in applicable environments (subregulation (1)), does not require testing by a competent person, and does not require maintaining testing records with the specified details (subregulations (3)-(4)). Therefore it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Add requirements to the policy/procedure to: (1) identify electrical equipment that is socket-outlet supplied and used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/chemicals/dust) and ensure it is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person at defined intervals; (2) ensure new/unused equipment is inspected for obvious damage before first use (explicitly stated); (3) keep testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date; and (4) allow/require tagging of equipment to evidence testing and next due date.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical equipment” (and exclusions) for the purposes of the regulatory Part. The policy governs procurement/commissioning controls for electrical equipment by requiring compliance with applicable Australian Standards (or recognised equivalents), inspection (especially for internationally sourced equipment), and corrective actions (return/modify/certify) if unsafe. These controls are functionally aligned with ensuring equipment within the provision’s scope is safe and standards-compliant prior to commissioning. The policy does not conflict with the provision’s definition and is broadly consistent with applying compliance checks to equipment that meets the defined criteria.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific legal obligation on a PCBU carrying out construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction and demolition sites. The policy addresses ensuring purchased electrical equipment meets applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) and inspection/certification of equipment, which is related to electrical safety but does not require or reference compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019, nor does it establish controls to ensure construction-site electrical installations meet that standard. Therefore, the policy does not fully satisfy the provision’s specific requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a construction-work electrical installations procedure) to explicitly require compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for construction and demolition sites, including responsibilities, verification/inspection/testing regimes, and documentation to demonstrate compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019 as applied under the Regulations.

matches.target_title: 15 References to standards
matches.target_text:
(1) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the words "Australian Standard" or the letters "AS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published by or on behalf of Standards Australia.

(2) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the expression "Australian/New Zealand Standard" or "AS/NZS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published jointly by or on behalf of Standards Australia and the Standards Council of New Zealand.

Notes

1 A person is only required to comply with an Australian Standard or Australian/New Zealand Standard that is applied, adopted or incorporated by, or otherwise referred to in, a regulation to the extent the regulation provides, whether expressly or by necessary implication. For example, in regulation 5, definition boiler, there is an exception in paragraph (e)(iii). The definition does not require a person to comply with AS 2593:2021, but equipment must be certified in compliance with AS 2593:2021 in order to fall within that particular exception. The reference to an Australian Standard in this case is part of a description of equipment excluded from the definition boiler. As a result, the equipment is not covered by particular provisions of these Regulations relating to high risk work.

2 See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical equipment to meet applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) or equivalent international standards recognised in Australia before commissioning, and to inspect/engage an electrician and remediate (return/modify/certify) if unsafe. The PROVISION defines what references to “AS” and “AS/NZS” mean within the Regulations and clarifies that compliance with a Standard is only required where a regulation applies/adopts/incorporates/refers to it. The POLICY’s use of AS/NZS references aligns with the PROVISION’s definitional framework and does not contradict it; it is a stricter internal control that supports meeting any regulatory obligations where Standards are incorporated by regulation.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses ensuring electrical equipment meets Australian Standards before commissioning, inspecting newly acquired equipment (especially international purchases), and returning/modifying/certifying equipment if it is electrically unsafe. The provision requires that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace is disconnected/isolated from supply and, once disconnected, is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or is replaced/permanently removed. The policy does not explicitly require immediate disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment, nor does it prohibit reconnection until repair/testing confirms safety, nor does it require replacement/permanent removal as an alternative control. Therefore it does not fully meet the operational control requirements of the provision for handling unsafe equipment at the workplace.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to explicitly require that any electrical equipment suspected or believed on reasonable grounds to be unsafe is immediately disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent use, and not reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and found safe by a competent person; alternatively require replacement or permanent removal from use. Include clear responsibilities, steps for isolation/lockout-tagout, and documentation/verification requirements.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy establishes controls to manage electrical risk specifically for electrical equipment procurement and commissioning: ensuring equipment meets applicable Australian Standards (or recognised equivalents) before commissioning, inspecting newly acquired (especially internationally purchased) equipment, engaging an electrician where doubt exists, and returning or modifying/certifying unsafe equipment. These measures are consistent with risk management for electrical equipment and align with managing electrical risks associated with design/construction/installation and related lifecycle considerations referenced in the provision.

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses procurement/commissioning safety by requiring electrical equipment to meet Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), inspection of newly acquired (especially imported) equipment, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The PROVISION imposes an operational control requirement in specified higher-risk use circumstances: the PCBU must minimise electrical risk from socket-outlet supply by using an appropriate residual current device (RCD), generally with ≤30 mA trip current for socket outlets ≤20 A, subject to stated exceptions. The POLICY does not mention RCD use, the specific triggering circumstances (moisture/heat/vibration/movement/amusement devices), the 30 mA requirement, or the exceptions. Therefore, while related to electrical safety, the POLICY does not fulfill the PROVISION’s specific obligation to ensure appropriate RCD protection in the listed circumstances.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to require that, in the circumstances listed in the regulation (harsh environments, equipment moved between locations, frequently moved equipment, amusement devices), electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets must be protected by an appropriate RCD, and specify that for socket outlets ≤20 A the RCD trip current must be ≤30 mA. Include the regulation’s exceptions (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer equivalent protection, non-earthed isolated generator equivalent protection) and assign responsibility for verifying/maintaining RCD protection (e.g., electrician verification, periodic testing/inspection).

source_text:
Modifications made to electrical equipment such as repairing electrical wiring or replacing faulty parts must only be carried out by a competent person.
Upon completion of the work, the equipment must be electrically tested and tagged and certified prior to it being commissioned.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The policy requires modifications/repairs be done by a competent person and requires testing/tagging/certification only upon completion prior to commissioning. It does not require pre-work testing to confirm de-energised status, nor does it address treating exposed parts as energised until isolation/testing, or earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore the policy does not meet the provision’s pre-work safety testing and handling requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to require that, before any electrical work begins, a competent person tests the equipment to confirm whether it is energised (and documents the result). Add requirements that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and that any high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Reference or align procedures with the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., per the relevant regulations/standards) and ensure worker training and records support these steps.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses electrical work on equipment (repairs/modifications) by a competent person and requires post-work electrical testing, tagging, and certification before commissioning. The PROVISION requires that certain electrical equipment (plug-in equipment used in harsh/damaging environments) be regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and that records of testing be kept with specified details (name, date, outcome, next test date), potentially via a tag. While the POLICY partially aligns (competent person; testing and tagging), it does not ensure (1) regular inspection/testing based on the specified risk/environment criteria, (2) inspection of new/unused equipment for obvious damage before first use, or (3) retention of testing records until next test/removal with all required record elements. Therefore the POLICY does not fully meet the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) require regular inspection and testing (not only after repairs) for plug-in electrical equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosives, dust), with defined intervals or a process to set intervals; (2) require pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (3) require keeping testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed, and ensure records include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (tag acceptable); and (4) explicitly prohibit use of unsafe electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.
