(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses electrical equipment modifications/repairs by requiring a competent person and post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The PROVISION imposes additional and more specific duties for electrical work on energised electrical equipment: it must be performed by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools, testing equipment and PPE; in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) meeting specified content requirements; and (subject to exceptions) with a qualified safety observer present. The POLICY does not mention energised work controls, SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements, or ensuring proper use of tools/PPE. Therefore, while partially aligned on competency, it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to: (1) require suitable, properly tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) require and define SWMS preparation and minimum contents (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); (3) require a competent safety observer with current (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation competency unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies; and (4) require the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY governs modifications/repairs to electrical equipment, requiring (i) work only by a competent person and (ii) post-work electrical testing, tagging, and certification before commissioning. The PROVISION sets mandatory preconditions before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences, including a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person, ensuring clear access/egress and isolation point accessibility/labeling/operability, and formal authorisation after consultation. The POLICY partially aligns with the competency concept but does not address the PROVISION’s required pre-work controls (risk assessment and recording, site/access conditions, isolation point requirements, and authorisation/consultation). Therefore, the POLICY does not fully satisfy the PROVISION’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure for energised electrical work to require, before commencement: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (or document applicability of the stated exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Retain existing requirements for competent persons and post-work testing/tagging/certification.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY sets requirements for electrical modifications to equipment: (1) work must be done by a competent person, and (2) after completion the equipment must be electrically tested, tagged, and certified before commissioning. The PROVISION requires the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out by a competent person and in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual. It also adds a specific competency requirement for detailed inspections involving electrical installations (must be qualified or assisted by a qualified person). While the POLICY aligns with the 'competent person' concept and includes post-work testing, it does not address the PROVISION’s additional requirements: ensuring work is performed in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual, and ensuring electrical-installation inspection is performed by a qualified person (or assisted by one). Therefore, the POLICY is not fully compliant with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to: (1) explicitly require maintenance/inspection/testing to be performed in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; and (2) specify that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation must be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person. Retain the existing requirements for competent persons and post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification prior to commissioning.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes 'electrical work' and lists exclusions where work is not considered electrical work (e.g., replacing a fuse/light bulb, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, plug-and-socket connection). The policy requires that modifications/repairs/replacements to electrical equipment be carried out only by a competent person and that, after completion, the equipment must be electrically tested, tagged, and certified prior to commissioning. This policy applies broadly to modifications such as repairing wiring or replacing faulty parts, without acknowledging the provision’s exclusions where certain tasks may be safely performed by non-experts and may not constitute electrical work. Therefore, the policy imposes stricter requirements than the provision and may be inconsistent with the regulatory definition/exclusions, creating a potential overreach and misclassification of non-electrical work as electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to align with the provision’s definition and exclusions by: (1) explicitly defining 'electrical work' consistent with the provision; (2) carving out excluded tasks (e.g., replacing fuses/light bulbs, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, plug-and-socket connection) from the 'competent person' and testing/tagging/certification requirements where appropriate; and (3) specifying that testing/tagging/certification applies when the activity is electrical work or otherwise creates electrical risk, and clarify supervision/competency requirements consistent with licensed/registered worker supervision where required.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found safe, or else replaced/removed. The policy addresses who may perform modifications/repairs (competent person) and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning, which partially aligns with the 'tested and found safe' concept. However, the policy does not explicitly require (1) identifying/handling 'unsafe electrical equipment' as defined by reasonable grounds, (2) immediate disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment, or (3) prohibiting reconnection until repaired or tested safe, or replacement/removal. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the provision’s control requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that any electrical equipment suspected/known to be unsafe is immediately disconnected/isolated from supply, prevented from being reconnected, and only returned to service after repair or testing confirms it is safe; otherwise it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Include criteria/process for determining 'reasonable grounds' of unsafety and controls (lockout/tagout, signage, access restriction) to prevent reconnection.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires ensuring de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while electrical work is being carried out (i.e., isolation/lockout controls during the work). The policy addresses who may perform modifications (competent person) and post-work testing/tagging/certification prior to commissioning, but it does not state any requirement or controls to prevent inadvertent re-energisation during the work. Therefore, the policy does not cover the provision’s core obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy/procedure to require isolation controls during electrical work, such as: de-energise and isolate the equipment; apply lockout/tagout (LOTO) or equivalent securing of isolators; verify isolation (test for dead) before starting work; control keys/access to isolation points; display warning tags/signage; and require re-energisation only under an authorised process after work completion.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning: The policy addresses modifications/repairs to electrical equipment and requires such work be done by a competent person, which aligns with the provision’s requirement that maintenance/inspection/testing of plant be carried out by a competent person. However, the provision also requires that maintenance, inspection and testing be carried out in accordance with the manufacturer’s recommendations (or, if none, a competent person’s recommendations; or annual inspection if not reasonably practicable). The policy does not state that maintenance/inspection/testing will follow manufacturer recommendations (or an equivalent hierarchy) nor does it set any inspection/testing frequency fallback. Additionally, the provision places the duty on the person with management or control of the plant to ensure these activities occur; the policy does not explicitly assign this responsibility.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to: (1) explicitly require maintenance, inspection and testing to be performed in accordance with manufacturer recommendations, or if unavailable, per a competent person’s recommendations, with an annual inspection minimum where compliance with those recommendations is not reasonably practicable; and (2) clearly assign responsibility to the person with management or control of the plant to ensure these activities are carried out.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The policy governs modifications/repairs to electrical equipment and requires work by a competent person plus post-work testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The provision regulates when electrical work on energised electrical equipment may occur, requiring it not be carried out unless specific necessity conditions apply (health/safety necessity, need for energisation to do the work properly, testing required under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The policy does not address or restrict energised electrical work to only those permitted circumstances, so it does not ensure the prohibition/conditional allowance in the provision is met.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment except where one of the permitted conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Add requirements for de-energisation/isolation as the default, documented justification/approval for any energised work, and controls such as risk assessment, safe work method, and competent/authorised personnel.

source_text:
It is important that electrical equipment is made safe before it is disposed of to prevent any risk of injury to workers or public when handling the equipment.

Prior to disposing of electrical equipment, consider the following:

 How can the equipment be made safe and ready for disposal i.e. can the electrical cord be cut/removed to prevent further use?   
 Does the equipment contain chemicals or radiation sources that need to be managed before it is disposed?   
 Can the equipment be disposed of through an e-waste stream?   
 Is there an alternate energy source within the equipment that retains residual energy, i.e. a capacitor?

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply and, once disconnected, is not reconnected until repaired/tested safe or is replaced/permanently removed from use. The policy focuses on making equipment safe before disposal (e.g., cutting/removing cords, managing chemicals/radiation, e-waste stream, residual energy) but does not explicitly require disconnection/isolating from supply, controls to prevent reconnection, or the required outcomes of repair/test-and-verify-safe versus replacement/permanent removal from use. Because these mandatory controls are not stated, the policy does not fully meet the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment is immediately disconnected/isolated from its electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and either (1) not reconnected until repaired or tested and confirmed safe, or (2) replaced or permanently removed from use (including disposal). Define/align criteria for 'unsafe' (reasonable grounds) and include procedural steps and responsibilities for isolation, tagging, and verification.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., removing cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, residual energy, using e-waste streams). The PROVISION imposes obligations to (1) regularly inspect and test certain electrical equipment used in damaging environments, (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, and (3) keep specified testing records until the equipment is next tested or removed/disposed of. While both concern electrical equipment safety and disposal, the POLICY does not cover the required inspection/testing regime, does not address the conditions triggering mandatory testing, and does not require keeping testing records with the specified content until disposal/removal. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy/procedure to: (1) require regular inspection and testing by a competent person for plug-in electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals, dust); (2) require inspection for obvious damage before first use of new/unused equipment; (3) require that testing records are kept until the next test or until the equipment is permanently removed/disposed of; and (4) specify record contents (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and allow tagging as the record format.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The policy addresses managing electrical risks specifically in the context of disposing of electrical equipment by requiring equipment to be made safe before disposal and prompting consideration of key electrical and related hazards (e.g., disabling further use by removing/cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, residual energy in capacitors, and appropriate e-waste disposal). This constitutes a risk-management approach aligned with the duty to manage electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY requires electrical equipment to be made safe before disposal (e.g., cutting/removing cords, managing residual energy such as capacitors, and addressing chemicals/radiation sources). The PROVISION defines what constitutes “electrical work” (e.g., disconnecting supply wiring, removing/maintaining electrical equipment) and lists exclusions for non-electrical tasks or simple replacements. Actions contemplated by the POLICY—such as cutting/removing an electrical cord, disconnecting wiring, or otherwise modifying equipment to prevent further use—may constitute “electrical work” under the PROVISION unless they fall within an exclusion. The POLICY does not specify that such tasks must be performed by a licensed/registered electrical worker (or under required supervision), nor does it distinguish between tasks that are electrical work versus excluded tasks. Therefore, the POLICY is not aligned with the PROVISION’s regulatory boundary conditions for who may perform electrical work and under what supervision.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY to: (1) explicitly identify that cord removal/cutting, disconnection of supply wiring, and any internal discharge/isolation activities may be “electrical work”; (2) require these activities to be performed only by appropriately licensed/registered electrical workers or under the supervision conditions permitted by the jurisdiction; (3) clarify which disposal-prep tasks are non-electrical (and permissible for non-electricians) versus electrical work; and (4) add a procedural control (e.g., pre-disposal electrical risk assessment and sign-off by a licensed/registered electrical worker where electrical work is involved).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

source_text:
A person in control of electrical equipment at the workplace must ensure that the electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if it is:

 supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
 is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes it to an environment that are likely to result in damage to that equipment or it reduces its expected life (e.g. conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust.)

Visual inspections of electrical equipment should be carried out each time the equipment is used to help determine whether it is safe. Regular visual inspections can identify any signs of damage, wear and tear or other defects and if present (even if there is doubt), must be isolated or taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe.

Scheduled testing and tagging of electrical equipment can detect electrical faults and deterioration that cannot be detected by visual inspection. The frequency of testing must be conducted in accordance with AS3760 In-service safety inspection and testing of electrical equipment.

New Equipment

Brand-new electrical equipment that has never been put into use does not have to be tested before its first use. However, a ‘new to service’ tag must be attached to the equipment by a person with control or management of that equipment before it goes into service.

It is advisable to conduct a visual inspection of the electrical equipment to ensure that it hasn’t been damaged during transportation, delivery, installation or commissioning.

Once new electrical equipment has been connected for use, it will require testing and tagging at the next scheduled test interval.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is clearly intended to implement the PROVISION’s requirements for inspection/testing of socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments, and it addresses new/unused equipment. However, it does not include the PROVISION’s mandatory recordkeeping requirements for testing (keeping records until next test or removal/disposal) nor the required record contents (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and that the record may be a tag. Additionally, for new and unused equipment, the PROVISION requires inspection for obvious damage before use; the POLICY only states this is “advisable,” which is weaker than the mandatory requirement. The POLICY also introduces a 'new to service' tag requirement, which is not required by the PROVISION, but that does not cure the missing mandatory elements.
remediation: Add a mandatory requirement to inspect new and unused electrical equipment for obvious damage before first use (replace “advisable” with “must”).
Add a mandatory recordkeeping section requiring records of any testing to be kept until the equipment is next tested or permanently removed/disposed.
Specify required record fields: tester’s name, date of testing, outcome, and next test due date; state that the record may be a tag attached to the equipment.
Ensure procedures/forms/tags used in practice capture and retain the required information for the required duration.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because both impose duties on the person with management/control to ensure inspection and (where needed) testing is performed by a competent person. However, the POLICY is narrower and does not fully meet the PROVISION’s requirements for “plant” generally and for maintenance/inspection/testing to be carried out in accordance with (a) manufacturer recommendations, or (b) competent person recommendations where manufacturer guidance is absent, or (c) at least annual inspection where (a) or (b) are not reasonably practicable. The POLICY focuses on electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets in damaging environments, sets testing frequency by AS3760, and includes visual checks each use and tagging rules for new equipment. It does not explicitly require maintenance (beyond isolation/repair), does not require following manufacturer recommendations (or competent-person recommendations where none exist), and does not include the fallback requirement of annual inspection when (a)/(b) are not reasonably practicable. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Amend the POLICY to: (1) explicitly cover all relevant “plant” (or clearly define scope and add a separate procedure for other plant) and require maintenance, inspection and testing (where necessary) by a competent person; (2) require maintenance/inspection/testing to follow manufacturer recommendations where available; (3) where manufacturer recommendations are absent, require following recommendations of a competent person; (4) include a documented fallback that inspection is performed at least annually where compliance with (2) or (3) is not reasonably practicable; and (5) ensure AS3760-based intervals are applied only where consistent with manufacturer/competent-person recommendations or document how conflicts are resolved.

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision prohibits use of electrical equipment that is required to be tested (under reg 150) but has not been tested. The policy establishes a system requiring regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in hostile/damaging environments, requires isolation/out-of-service where defects are suspected, and mandates scheduled testing/tagging per AS3760. It also clarifies that brand-new equipment need not be tested before first use but must be tagged 'new to service' and then tested at the next scheduled interval. This framework supports ensuring equipment that requires testing is tested before continued use, aligning with the provision’s prohibition on using untested equipment when testing is required.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific obligations regarding residual current devices (RCDs): (1) ensure RCDs are tested regularly by a competent person, and (2) keep records of all RCD testing (other than daily testing) until the next test or removal from use. The policy text addresses inspection/testing and tagging of electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets in damaging environments, visual inspections, and testing frequency per AS3760, but it does not explicitly require regular testing of RCDs nor does it require maintaining records of RCD testing as specified. Therefore, the policy does not fully cover the provision’s RCD testing and recordkeeping requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to explicitly require: (1) regular testing of all workplace RCDs by a competent person to confirm effective operation (including defining/aligning the testing interval with applicable requirements/standards), and (2) retention of records for all non-daily RCD tests until the RCD is next tested or permanently removed from use. Include responsibility assignment (management/control), record format/location, and retention/availability requirements.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY is functionally related to the PROVISION because it sets procedures for identifying potentially unsafe electrical equipment (via visual inspection each use and scheduled testing/tagging) and requires that equipment showing signs of damage/defects (or where there is doubt) be isolated/taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe. This aligns with the PROVISION’s requirement to disconnect/isolate unsafe electrical equipment and ensure it is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. The POLICY’s threshold of isolating equipment when defects are present or even if there is doubt is consistent with the PROVISION’s definition of unsafe as having reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per WHS risk management requirements). The policy establishes a risk control system for electrical risks by requiring regular inspection, testing and tagging by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments; requiring pre-use visual checks and isolation of suspect equipment; and setting testing frequency to AS/NZS 3760. These measures directly address electrical risks associated with maintenance and testing of electrical equipment, aligning with the provision’s obligation to manage electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

source_title: 9.1 Business Unit and Site Electrical Safety Officers
source_text:
The primary purpose of an ESO is to provide advice and support to the business unit regarding CSIRO electrical work requirements and safe electrical work practices.

The ESO is not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activites on site and/or across a business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function MUST appoint a Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) at each site where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members or affiliates within the business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function may also appoint a Business Unit Electrical Safety Officer (BU ESO) where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members and affiliates across multiple sites within the business unit.

Note: A Site ESO does not need to be appointed for work on extra low voltage equipment or installations.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before energised electrical work starts (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; work area and isolation point access/egress; isolation point marked/labelled and operable quickly; and formal authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace). The policy text is about appointing Electrical Safety Officers (Site ESO/BU ESO) and explicitly states the ESO is not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activities. It does not set out any requirements or process to ensure the provision’s preconditions (risk assessment/recording, access/labeling/isolation operability, consultation, and authorisation) are met. Therefore, while related to electrical safety governance, the policy does not demonstrate controls to satisfy the provision’s obligations for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Add or reference a procedure for energised electrical work that requires, before commencement: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area is clear for access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (including handling of the stated exception); and (4) documented authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Clarify roles/responsibilities (e.g., who performs the risk assessment, who verifies site conditions, and who authorises) consistent with the policy’s statement that ESOs do not approve/manage work.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines the scope of what counts as electrical equipment/installation for a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU): those under the person's management or control. The policy establishes an internal governance role (ESO) to advise on electrical work requirements and safe practices at sites where recurring electrical work occurs, which is a mechanism relevant to managing electrical work associated with equipment/installations under CSIRO/business unit control. The policy does not contradict the provision and is consistent with the concept that responsibilities attach to what is under the organization's management or control.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace (a substantive duty to implement risk management controls). The policy text primarily defines the purpose and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) and explicitly states ESOs are not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activities. While appointing ESOs can support risk management, the policy excerpt does not assign responsibility or describe processes to ensure electrical risks are managed in accordance with the required risk management framework (e.g., identification, assessment, control, review per Part 3.1). Therefore, based on the provided excerpt alone, the policy does not demonstrate fulfillment of the duty to manage electrical risks.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy (or reference a linked procedure) to explicitly require the business unit/PCBU to manage electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1, including: hazard identification, risk assessment (where required), implementation of controls (e.g., design/installation/maintenance/testing controls), verification and review of control effectiveness, and clear assignment of accountable roles (e.g., senior manager/site management) for managing and approving electrical work and safety activities, with ESOs providing advisory support.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. The policy sets requirements for appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) where “recurring electrical work” is carried out, and provides an exclusion only for “extra low voltage equipment or installations.” Because the policy’s trigger (“recurring electrical work”) depends on the legal/regulated definition of electrical work, a compliance check is necessary. The policy is not compliant/aligned because it does not incorporate or reference the provision’s exclusions from “electrical work” (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, certain supervised assistance, etc.). As written, the policy could require ESO appointments for activities that the provision explicitly says are not electrical work, creating overbroad obligations and potential confusion/inconsistency. Additionally, the policy’s only stated exemption (extra low voltage) is not mapped to the provision’s definition/exclusions and may omit other non-electrical-work scenarios recognized by the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the policy to align the trigger for ESO appointment with the provision’s definition of “electrical work,” including explicitly adopting or cross-referencing the listed exclusions. Clarify that ESO appointment is required only where recurring activities meet the defined scope of electrical work, and document how extra-low-voltage work is treated relative to the jurisdictional definition/exclusions.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

source_text:
Individuals appointed to a BU or Site ESO role must have a level of training, qualification, licencing, etc that is commensurate to the competencies required for electrical work that is carried out on the site they primarily work at.

Example - ESO competency

CASS conducts electrical work on 2 different sites Marsfield and Narrabri.

At Marsfield they conduct work on portable electrical equipment and at Narrabri they conduct work on fixed and portable electrical equipment. Based on this information, a BU or Site ESO located at:

 Marsfield will need to have completed an electrical competency assessment.   
 Narrabri will need to hold an appropriate electrical licence.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The POLICY addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for individuals appointed as BU or Site ESO, ensuring their competency is commensurate with the electrical work performed at their primary site. The PROVISION imposes duties on the PCBU specifically for electrical work on energised electrical equipment, requiring that such work be carried out by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and (subject to exceptions) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. While the POLICY partially aligns with the 'competent person' concept (via training/assessment/licensing), it does not address key mandatory elements of the PROVISION: (1) ensuring suitable/tested/maintained tools, testing equipment and PPE; (2) ensuring the work is performed in accordance with a SWMS meeting specified content requirements; (3) ensuring a safety observer is present when required and meets competence and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment requirements; and (4) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/PPE. Therefore, the POLICY is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the PROVISION.
compliance.remediation: Update the POLICY (or add linked procedures) to explicitly require for energised electrical work: (1) verification that the worker is competent for energised work; (2) provision and verification of suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (3) preparation and use of a SWMS that identifies the work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (4) appointment of a safety observer when required, including documented competence and rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months; (5) a risk assessment process for the testing-only exception; and (6) supervision/assurance measures to ensure workers properly use tools/testing equipment/PPE.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if:
