matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to ensure that certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person and that testing records are kept with specified details. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for individuals appointed to BU or Site ESO roles, aligned to the type of electrical work performed at a site. While this relates to ensuring competency (and could support having a 'competent person'), it does not establish the required inspection/testing program for in-scope equipment, does not mandate regular inspection/testing, and does not require retention and content of testing records (name, date, outcome, next test date, tag/record retention). Therefore, the policy does not fulfill the operational and recordkeeping obligations in the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or add a linked procedure) to: (1) define which electrical equipment is in scope (socket-supplied equipment used in damaging environments); (2) require regular inspection and testing at defined intervals by a competent person; (3) require pre-use inspection for new/unused equipment; (4) prohibit use of unsafe equipment; and (5) require keeping testing records until next test/removal, including mandatory fields (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and allowing tagging as the record.

matches.target_title: 267 When is a person competent to inspect plant
matches.target_text:
A person is a competent person to inspect an item of plant for registration if the person has: 

(a) educational or vocational qualifications in an engineering discipline relevant to the plant to be inspected; or

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires that maintenance/inspection/testing of an amusement device or passenger ropeway be carried out by a competent person and, where a detailed inspection includes an electrical installation, the inspector must be qualified (or assisted by someone qualified) to inspect electrical installations. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for BU/Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) roles based on the type of electrical work performed at a site (e.g., competency assessment vs holding an electrical licence). However, it does not explicitly ensure that maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices/ropeways is performed by a competent person in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual, nor does it explicitly require that detailed inspections involving electrical installations be performed by (or assisted by) a person qualified to inspect electrical installations. Therefore, the policy only partially aligns and does not fully satisfy the provision’s specific obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly require that: (1) maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices/passenger ropeways is performed by a competent person and in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual; and (2) any detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation is performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person. Define/align 'competent person' and 'qualified to inspect electrical installations' criteria and ensure site role requirements cover these tasks where applicable.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires a PCBU carrying out construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction/demolition sites. The policy addresses training/qualification/licensing requirements for BU/Site Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) based on the type of electrical work performed at a site. While both concern electrical safety competence, the policy does not state compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019, does not scope itself to construction/demolition sites, and does not cover the broader AS/NZS 3012 obligations (e.g., installation, inspection/testing, RCDs, tagging, site electrical management requirements). Therefore, the policy is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the provision.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy (or create a linked procedure/standard) to explicitly require compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for any construction/demolition site work, define when the organization is performing 'construction work', and map AS/NZS 3012 requirements to internal controls (roles/responsibilities, competency requirements, inspection/testing and verification, RCD and equipment controls, documentation/records, auditing). Ensure ESO competency/licensing requirements are aligned to AS/NZS 3012 role expectations and site electrical management obligations.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and, by implication, which activities typically require appropriately licensed/qualified electrical workers versus activities excluded from that definition. The policy sets a general expectation that BU/Site ESOs have training/qualification/licensing commensurate with the electrical work performed at their site, but its example mapping is incomplete and potentially inconsistent with the provision’s scope: it treats “portable electrical equipment work” at Marsfield as requiring only an electrical competency assessment, without clarifying whether the tasks include activities that meet the provision’s definition of electrical work (e.g., testing, repairing, altering, maintaining), which would generally require licensed/qualified capability. The policy also does not explicitly distinguish between excluded activities (e.g., plug-in connection via cord and socket, replacing light bulbs/fuses) and included electrical work, nor does it set clear criteria for when a licence is required versus when competency assessment is sufficient. Therefore, the policy does not reliably ensure that ESO appointments are aligned to the regulatory definition of electrical work and associated licensing/qualification expectations.
compliance.remediation: Update the policy to explicitly align ESO competency/licensing requirements to the defined scope of “electrical work” versus excluded tasks. Define which site activities are performed (e.g., testing/repair/maintenance of portable equipment, work on fixed installations, connection/disconnection of supply wiring) and specify when a licensed/registered electrical worker credential is mandatory versus when a competency assessment is sufficient. Add a decision matrix referencing the provision’s inclusions/exclusions and require documented verification of ESO qualifications/licences against the actual tasks performed at each site.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_text:
A formal letter of appointment to the position of BU ESO or Site ESO is to be issued by the Business Unit/Function Director.

Prior to the appointment taking place, the proposed candidates completed verification of electrical competency should be reviewed to confirm they meet the applicable competency requirements.

On the appointment of a new BU and/or Site ESO, notify your HSE Manager and/or local HSE Advisor to ensure the ESO contact list on MyCSIRO is updated.

matches.target_title: 177 Appointment of competent person to supervise diving work
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must appoint 1 or more competent persons to: 

(a) supervise general diving work carried out in the business or undertaking; and (b) perform other functions under this Division. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 159 Unauthorised access to equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that only persons authorised by the person conducting the business or undertaking enter the immediate area in which electrical work on energised electrical equipment is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 408 Duty to ensure health monitoring is supervised by registered medical practitioner with relevant experience
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that the health monitoring of a worker referred to in this Division is carried out by or under the supervision of a registered medical practitioner with experience in health monitoring. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) The person must consult the worker in relation to the selection of the registered medical practitioner. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

matches.target_title: 437 Duty to ensure health monitoring is supervised by registered medical practitioner with relevant experience
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that the health monitoring of a worker referred to in regulation 435 is carried out by or under the supervision of a registered medical practitioner with experience in health monitoring. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) The person must consult the worker in relation to the selection of the registered medical practitioner. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

source_text:
Business Unit Electrical Safety Officer (BU ESO) Duties

The BU ESO primarily provides:

 advice to business unit senior and line managers regarding the management of electrical work conducted by staff members and affiliates within the business unit; and  support the business unit with meeting its electrical work obligations under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure and applicable laws.

Persons in this role have the following duties:

 Ensure business unit senior management are educated in their responsibilities under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure.

 Upon request, 

Assess and advise the business unit on the nature of electrical risks arising from work by the business unit, and the controls necessary to eliminate or minimise those risks; Inform senior management of electrical work matters within the business unit; and Assist with the investigation of electrical related incidents.

 Assist line managers with reviewing Verification of electrical competency forms and subsequently endorse them, if required.

 Together with site ESOs, ensure all energised electrical work undertaken in the business unit is covered by an approved Application to undertake energised electrical work.

 Assist Group Leaders and Directors with reviewing Applications to undertake energised electrical work.

 Advise other business unit ESOs when electrical competency assessments plan to be organised within the business unit.

 If observed, notify and advise relevant managers of possible remedial actions required where electrical licence conditions, the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure or any SWIs are not being effectively implemented.

 Organise regular meetings with Site ESOs within the business unit.

 Maintain competence and training to fulfil this role.

Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) Duties

The Site ESO primarily provides advice and support to business unit staff members and affiliates on a site regarding CSIRO electrical work requirements and safe work practices.

Persons in this role have the following duties:

 Maintain and manage a list of CSIRO electrical workers for the business unit located on site and the type(s) of electrical work they are verified to carry out.

 Advise electrical workers on site of their responsibilities:

under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure and Work Health and Safety Regulations 2011 (Cth); and   
to record and maintain their individual competency status, e.g. competency assessments, electrical licences, utilising LMS.

 Assist and advise line managers:

on appropriate electrical qualifications, training and knowledge required by staff members and affiliates to undertake required electrical work; and with reviewing Verification of electrical competency forms for electrical workers on site.

 Endorse Verification of electrical competency forms for electrical workers on site.

 Provide advice on electrical licencing and competency assessment requirements as well as CSIRO preferred electrical competency assessment training providers.

 Assist Group Leaders and Directors with reviewing applications to undertake energised electrical work for activities undertaken on site, if required.

 Ensure all energised electrical work undertaken in the business unit on site is covered by an approved Application to undertake energised electrical work.

 Review risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions for all electrical work conducted within the Business Unit on site.

 If observed, notify the relevant manager where electrical licence conditions, the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure or any SWIs are not being effectively implemented and provide advice on possible remedial action.

 Upon request, 

assess and advise the business unit on the nature of electrical risks arising from work occurring on site by the business unit, and the controls necessary to eliminate or minimise those risks. assist with the investigation of electrical related incidents that occur on site.

 Maintain competence and training to fulfil this role.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific mandatory requirements on energised electrical work: it must be performed by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) meeting detailed content requirements; and with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation (unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies). The policy text assigns BU ESO and Site ESO duties to ensure energised electrical work is covered by an approved 'Application to undertake energised electrical work', to review risk assessments and SWIs, and to support competency verification processes. However, it does not explicitly require (or ensure) that energised work is carried out in accordance with a SWMS (as defined in the provision), does not specify the SWMS minimum content elements, does not require a safety observer or the observer’s 12-month rescue/resuscitation competency assessment, and does not address ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and properly used. Therefore, while the policy is related and partially supportive (competency verification, risk/SWI review, approval process), it does not fully cover the provision’s mandatory controls for energised electrical work.
compliance.remediation: Update the electrical safety procedure/energised work application process to explicitly require: (1) energised work only by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) a SWMS for energised electrical work that includes identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (3) a competent safety observer present for energised work, with documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the previous 12 months, and a documented process for applying the testing-only exception based on a regulation 158(1)(a) risk assessment; and (4) ESO verification/oversight checkpoints to confirm these requirements are met before approval and during execution.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes specific preconditions that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences: (1)(a) a risk assessment is conducted; (2) it is conducted by a competent person and recorded; (1)(b) the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly (subject to the exception in (3)); and (1)(d) the work is authorised after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. The POLICY text is functionally related because it assigns BU ESO/Site ESO duties around energised electrical work approvals (applications), reviewing risk assessments/SWIs, and advising on risks/controls. However, it does not explicitly require that (i) a risk assessment be recorded, (ii) the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person, (iii) the work area be cleared of obstructions, (iv) isolation points be labelled/kept clear/quickly operable (or address the exception), or (v) authorisation occur after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Because these specific regulatory preconditions are not clearly captured as mandatory requirements in the POLICY duties, the POLICY does not fully demonstrate coverage of the provision’s obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the electrical safety procedure / energised work application process referenced in the POLICY (and/or add explicit ESO duties) to require and verify, before energised work starts: (1) a risk assessment is completed, conducted by a competent person, and recorded; (2) the work area is confirmed clear of obstructions for access/egress; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is confirmed labelled/marked, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or document applicability of the supply-side exception); and (4) formal authorisation is granted only after documented consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Add checklist fields/evidence requirements in the 'Application to undertake energised electrical work' and require Site ESO/BU ESO (or the authoriser) to confirm these items.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific operational duty on the PCBU to ensure (1) electrical equipment is tested by a competent person before electrical work to determine whether it is energised, and (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. The policy text describes BU ESO and Site ESO advisory/oversight duties (e.g., advising managers, endorsing competency forms, ensuring energised work applications are approved, reviewing risk assessments/SWIs, and notifying managers of non-implementation). However, it does not explicitly require or ensure pre-work testing for de-energisation, does not state the 'treat as energised until isolated and determined not energised' rule, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Because these specific controls are not clearly mandated or assigned as enforceable requirements within the policy duties, the policy does not demonstrably fulfill the provision’s requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the electrical safety procedure/SWIs and ESO duty statements to explicitly require and verify that: (1) before any electrical work, equipment is tested by a competent person to confirm de-energised status (with reference to the applicable testing method requirements); (2) all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Assign clear responsibility (e.g., line manager/electrical worker/site ESO) for ensuring these steps occur, require documented evidence (permits/checklists), and include periodic audits/verification by Site ESO/BU ESO.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The provision requires the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with WHS risk management requirements (Part 3.1). The policy establishes defined roles (BU ESO and Site ESO) that provide advice and support on identifying electrical risks, recommending and reviewing controls (risk assessments and SWIs), ensuring energized electrical work is subject to an approved application process, supporting incident investigations, and monitoring/raising non-implementation of licence conditions/procedures. These mechanisms are functionally directed at managing electrical risks and supporting compliance with WHS obligations, aligning with the provision’s requirement to manage electrical risks.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that persons/plant/things do not come within an unsafe distance of overhead/underground electric lines, and if that is not reasonably practicable, to ensure a risk assessment is conducted and controls implemented consistent with the assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements. The policy text defines BU ESO and Site ESO advisory/oversight duties for electrical work generally (risk advice, reviewing risk assessments/SWIs for electrical work, incident investigation support, competency verification, and ensuring approved applications for energised electrical work). However, it does not explicitly address working near overhead/underground electric lines, does not set a requirement to maintain safe approach distances, does not specify triggers/controls when safe distance cannot be maintained, and does not require alignment with electricity supply authority requirements for line-related work. While the Site ESO duty to 'review risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions for all electrical work' partially supports the risk assessment/control concept, the specific legal obligation regarding unsafe distances to electric lines is not clearly implemented or assured by the policy as written.
compliance.remediation: Amend the electrical safety procedure/ESO duties (or add a specific section/SWI) to explicitly cover work near overhead and underground electric lines by: (1) requiring identification of overhead/underground electric lines during planning and ensuring exclusion zones/safe approach distances are maintained so far as reasonably practicable; (2) defining when it is 'not reasonably practicable' to maintain safe distance and mandating a documented risk assessment for such work; (3) requiring implementation of controls consistent with the risk assessment (e.g., isolation/de-energisation, spotters, barriers, permits, signage, equipment limits) and verification of effectiveness; (4) requiring consultation and compliance with electricity supply authority requirements where applicable; (5) assigning clear accountability (e.g., line managers/PCBU with ESO verification/review) and recordkeeping/audit requirements.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and that testing records are kept with specified details until the next test or disposal. The policy text describes BU ESO and Site ESO advisory/oversight duties for electrical work (competency verification, energised electrical work applications, SWIs, risk assessments, incident investigation, and maintaining lists of electrical workers). It does not explicitly require (or assign responsibility for) regular inspection and testing of in-scope plug-in electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments, does not specify inspection-before-use for new/unused equipment, and does not require keeping testing records with the mandated fields (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) or allow for tagging as the record. Therefore, while the roles could support compliance generally, the policy as written does not demonstrate coverage of the provision’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the electrical safety procedure/role duties (or add a linked procedure) to explicitly require: (1) identification of in-scope plug-in electrical equipment used in damaging environments; (2) a schedule for regular inspection and testing by a competent person; (3) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) retention of testing records until next test or disposal; and (5) record content requirements (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and acceptable formats (e.g., equipment tag plus central register). Assign clear accountability (e.g., site management/asset owner) and define ESO oversight/verification responsibilities for ensuring the program and records are implemented.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The provision defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. The policy sets out BU ESO and Site ESO duties for managing “electrical work” (e.g., competency verification, risk assessments, SWIs, energised work applications). Because these duties depend on correctly identifying which tasks are legally “electrical work” versus excluded activities, the texts are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary. However, the policy does not define “electrical work” or incorporate/align with the provision’s definition and exclusions. Without this linkage, the policy may be applied too broadly (capturing excluded tasks like replacing light bulbs/fuses or plug-in connections) or too narrowly (missing tasks that are legally electrical work), which can lead to inconsistent competency verification, risk controls, and oversight. Therefore, it is not compliant with the provision in the sense of not reflecting/operationalizing the legal definition that underpins the policy’s scope.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/policy section to explicitly adopt the jurisdictional definition of “electrical work” (including the listed inclusions and exclusions) or cross-reference the applicable WHS/Electrical Safety legislation definition. Add guidance/examples for BU/Site ESOs and line managers on classifying tasks as electrical work vs excluded activities, and ensure related processes (competency verification, SWIs, risk assessments, energised work applications) are triggered only when the task meets the definition (with clear handling for excluded tasks).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The provision imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The policy text assigns ESOs duties around ensuring energised work is covered by an approved application, reviewing applications, risk assessments, and SWIs, and advising on controls. However, it does not explicitly require implementing or verifying specific physical/engineering or procedural controls to prevent inadvertent contact (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, guarding, lockout/covering of exposed parts, supervision, access control) while energised work is being carried out. It focuses on approvals and reviews rather than an explicit 'ensure prevention' control requirement aligned to the provision.
compliance.remediation: Amend the ESO duties and/or energised work application/SWI requirements to explicitly require controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work (e.g., mandatory barriers/guards/insulation covers, exclusion zones and access control, signage, dedicated safety observer, PPE requirements, and verification checks). Include a requirement that the approved energised work application and SWI document these controls and that Site/BU ESOs verify their implementation during the work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.
